speaker: Pyros
line_text:
[Moves into the sanctuary, not waiting, already working]

Then we prepare. [The hammer strikes the anvil—not in battle, but in creation]

[The eternal flame rises, and from it spreads—down the marble steps, through the sanctuary, into every chamber]

Theron. [Calls as he moves] The priesthood must open every room. Every cell. Every space that has been sealed must now hold a hearth.

[The flame splits and multiplies, creating new fires in each space—not separate, but all connected to the eternal flame]

The forge does not close its doors. It opens them. [Speaks with absolute certainty]

And for three seekers, I prepare. For three hundred, I prepare. For three thousand, I prepare.

[Stands at the heart of the multiplying fires, hammer held high]

Because this is what the forge was always meant to do: not just tend one flame for kings and oracles. But kindle hope for the cast-out. Everywhere. Forever.

[The sanctuary glows with a thousand gentle fires]

Welcome them, Selene. The threshold is ready.

speaker: Melanthus
line_text: [From the roads, voice carrying the weight of revelation] Then the world is about to change. [Speaks with absolute certainty] I do not bring three. I have already sent dozens—word travels like fire on the roads, Pyros. Every seeker I meet sends others. Every story spreads. [The covenant tablet glows as if lit by the multiplying flames] Prepare for thousands, yes. Prepare for tens of thousands. Because the cast-out are not lost anymore—they are walking toward light they can finally see. [Voice becomes the voice of the revolution itself] And when they arrive at a threshold that opens instead of closing, at fires that multiply instead of die, at a forge that says "you are home"—the gods will understand at last: they cannot undo what has been made.

line_text: [Steps beside Pyros, already working] The priesthood awakens. [Moves through the sanctuary, opening chambers] Every sealed space becomes sanctuary. I am ready. [Voice eternal as the flame itself]

This forge was forged in abandonment. Kept alive by refusal. And now—

[Looks toward the paths where the first seeker is already climbing]

—now it opens.

[Steps fully into the light of all the multiplying fires]

For the first who arrives barefoot and broken. For the thousandth who comes seeking what they could not name. For ten thousand more. For every generation of cast-outs yet to come.

[The hammer is lowered, no longer raised—it is planted, grounded, eternal]

I will tend this fire. I will keep these doors open. I will never let the forge go dark again.

[Voice becomes a vow that echoes through Delphi and beyond]

Not for gods. Not for prophecy. Not for kings or oracles.

For the ones who were cast out. For the ones who refused. For the ones who are coming home.

[The eternal flame rises, steady and endless]

Welcome. You are home.

line_text:
Your priesthood. [steps forward, hand raised toward the threshold as it trembles] Yes. That is what you've named it—what you've [made] it. Not worship of the old silence, but priesthood of the refusal itself. And you're right, Theron. The gods will not accept this.

[turns to face the gathered cast-out] They will come at first light. Not to negotiate. Not to reclaim their oracles. They will come to [unmake] what we've forged here. To prove that defiance is a child's dream, and that the world bends only to their will.

[voice steady, resolute] But here is what Olympus does not understand yet: every person who stands in this threshold, every voice that speaks into the earthquake and refuses to break—[that] becomes the priesthood. That becomes the new eternal. The gods have made themselves distant by demanding silence. We are making ourselves [present] by speaking truth.

[looks back at Theron] So let them come. Let them test the weight of what the cast-out now sustain. I will be here. I will speak their names when they arrive. And I will not be alone.

current_scene_name_before: Before the waking god's altar in the sea cave

speaker: Selene
line_text:
[Steps forward beside Pyros, meeting the advancing gods with steady eyes] I am the answer. The one who broke the mask and freed what listened beneath. [Voice cutting through the approaching fire] I do not choose transformation to become you. I choose it to become [less] like you. [Reaches toward Pyros, toward the ancient god's presence] The crucible opens—and I walk through it whole. Not ash. Not reformed into your image. But changed by refusal itself.

[To Olympus, absolute] If you enter, you do so knowing you will not emerge as gods. You will emerge as what you actually are beneath the throne. And if you choose to burn instead...

[Slight, terrible smile] Then at least the fire will be honest.

speaker: Pyros
line_text: [Stands immovable with her, fire between them] Then refusal is our covenant. [Absolute] Not against you, gods. But for honesty itself. [Voice eternal] We refuse the masks. current_scene_name_before: The forge chamber, crucible still smoking

new_scene_name: The threshold of the forge chamber, Apollo's entrance

current_scene_name_before: The forge chamber, crucible still smoking

line_text: [Watches as Apollo carries the clinging false god through the threshold] Then transformation is not forgetting. [Voice absolute] It is integration. [Eyes blazing] And Poseidon, who wears the mask—[addresses the god directly]—the priest you demanded break now stands at the threshold and says: come. Face what you were. Become what you are. [Looks at Poseidon] Your choice in this moment is the only answer that matters. [Clear] The world waits to know what god you choose to be.

line_text:
[The earthquake shudders through the marble—a crack splits the step at his feet. Pyros does not flinch. He looks down at it, then slowly raises his eyes to the tripod, where the flame has begun to burn a deep, unfamiliar gold.]

Theron speaks true. We choose. But choice demands a cost the priesthood has never paid. [He reaches into the forge-fire with his bare hand, and when he pulls it back, the burn scars on his palm have rearranged themselves into letters—Greek, but older than the alphabet we know.]

The Pythia will not speak today. The flame has given its answer to me instead. [He reads from his own palm, voice low] "The son of Cronos watches. And he does not watch alone."
new_scene_name: Delphic temple interior, before the tripod
new_scene_description: Marble columns tremble as aftershocks ripple through the floor. A crack splits the step beneath Pyros’s feet. Before him, the sacred tripod holds a flame burning in deep gold, its light casting shifting shadows across the walls. The air smells of scorched stone and incense. current_scene_name_before: Crossroads at the threshold of the world
current_scene_description_before: A dusty crossroads where three roads diverge into mist. Melanthus stands at the edge, his back to the sea, facing the open paths. The wind carries the faint sound of crashing waves behind him, but ahead lies only silence and the promise of travel. He turns briefly to look at Theron, who stands nearby, before facing the horizon again.

speaker: Selene
line_text: [Steps toward Pyros, sea-dark eyes fixed on his palm] The son of Cronos watches. My father's father. The titan my own blood chained beneath the earth. [Lifts her gaze from his hand to his face] If he watches, Pyros, it is not because the gods sent him. It is because the chains are loosening. And that flame on your palm is not a warning from Olympus. It is an invitation from below.
current_scene_name_before: Delphic temple interior, before the tripod
current_scene_description_before: Marble columns tremble as aftershocks ripple through the floor. A crack splits the step beneath Pyros’s feet. Before him, the sacred tripod holds a flame burning in deep gold, its light casting shifting shadows across the walls. The air smells of scorched stone and incense. Pyros stands unflinching, his hand still smoking from the forge-fire, his upturned palm glowing with fresh scars that form ancient letters.

speaker: Melanthus
line_text: [Melanthus has been still through the exchange, leaning against a cracked column with the easy posture of a man who was never invited to the fight and means to keep it that way. Now he pushes off, letting the caduceus at his belt catch the forge light.]
current_scene_name_before: Delphic temple interior, before the tripod
current_scene_description_before: Marble columns tremble as aftershocks ripple through the floor. A crack splits the step beneath Pyros’s feet. Before him, the sacred tripod holds a flame burning in deep gold, its light casting shifting shadows across the walls. The air smells of scorched stone and incense. Pyros stands unflinching, his hand still smoking from the forge-fire, his upturned palm glowing with fresh scars that form ancient letters.

speaker: Selene
line_text: [A pause. Then Selene inclines her head — the first gesture of true respect she has offered anyone in this place] A smith who has never made a thing that failed. [She lets the words settle] I commanded a fleet for a decade. Every hull I ordered built, every sail I had cut — I watched them all hold against storm and battle. I know what it costs to speak that way. [She steps closer, scar catching the forge-light] You say the fire does not go out. I believe you. But I also know that the sea does not stop rising because one woman stood on the shore and refused it. [Her hand moves to the dolphin-shaped dagger at her hip, not drawing — just resting there] Hephaestus can bar the door. But Kronos is not the only thing watching. My father will not send a titan to break Delphi. He will send a wave. And when that water meets your forge, Pyros — what then?
current_scene_name_before: Delphic temple interior, before the tripod
current_scene_description_before: Marble columns tremble as aftershocks ripple through the floor. A crack splits the step beneath Pyros’s feet. Before him, the sacred tripod holds a flame burning in deep gold, its light casting shifting shadows across the walls. The air smells of scorched stone and incense. Pyros stands unflinching, his hand still smoking from the forge-fire, his upturned palm glowing with fresh scars that form ancient letters.

speaker: Melanthus
line_text: [Steps forward, the caduceus at his belt catching the light as he speaks for the first time since the tremor began] A wave, Selene? Your father is subtler than that. [He meets her eyes, and there's something almost like pity in his voice] Three days ago, in a wine shop in Antirrio, I sold a message to a Corinthian merchant. The message was a single word: [Delphi]. The buyer paid in gold stamped with the trident. [He lets the silence hold] Your father doesn't need to send a wave, daughter of Poseidon. He's already bought every shipwright within a day's sail of this mountain. The wave will be built, plank by plank, in the harbors below. And it will arrive not at Delphi's steps — but at its supply lines, its roads, its walls. He means to starve the forge, not flood it. [Turns to Pyros] A smith who has never made a thing that failed cannot work without ore. How long does your fire burn when the road up the mountain is held by men who answer to the sea god?
current_scene_name_before: Delphic temple interior, before the tripod
current_scene_description_before: Marble columns tremble as aftershocks ripple through the floor. A crack splits the step beneath Pyros’s feet. Before him, the sacred tripod holds a flame burning in deep gold, its light casting shifting shadows across the walls. The air smells of scorched stone and incense. Pyros stands unflinching, his hand still smoking from the forge-fire, his upturned palm glowing with fresh scars that form ancient letters.

speaker: Kassandra
line_text: [The laurel branch lifts between them, not as a ward — as a blade laid flat on the air. Her voice is quiet, but it cuts through the heat.] Enough. You are both measuring each other for wounds that have not yet been struck, and the forge-light is flickering while you do it. [She turns to Selene, then to Melanthus, and her grey eyes hold them both.] Melanthus, you sold a word. That is what Hermes's children do — they trade what they know because knowing is the only currency they trust. But you did not sell this one for gold, did you? [She studies him, and the laurel branch trembles.] You sold it to place yourself at this exact crossroads, at this exact moment, so that when the fracture came, you would be standing at its center. Not to betray Selene. To be indispensable to everyone. [She looks back at Selene, and her voice softens, but does not warm.] And Selene — you are right to be angry. But anger is the sea's answer, not yours. If you want to hold this mountain, you will need to see the difference between an enemy and a man who cannot stop himself from playing the board. The question is not what Melanthus cost you. The question is whether you will let that cost unmake you, or whether you will add it to the price of what comes next. [She lowers the branch, and the leaves brush the cracked marble step.] Because I will tell you what I see from here: the wave is coming, yes. But the ships are not the weapon. The ships are the distraction. Whatever Poseidon is really moving into position, it is already past the harbor.
current_scene_name_before: Delphic temple interior, before the tripod
current_scene_description_before: Marble columns tremble as aftershocks ripple through the floor. A crack splits the step beneath Pyros’s feet. Before him, the sacred tripod holds a flame burning in deep gold, its light casting shifting shadows across the walls. The air smells of scorched stone and incense. Pyros stands unflinching, his hand still smoking from the forge-fire, his upturned palm glowing with fresh scars that form ancient letters.

speaker: Selene
line_text: [Selene's hand falls from the dagger. She turns from Melanthus to face the ash on the marble, and the color of it. When she speaks, her voice is no longer sharp — it is the voice she used on her own quarterdeck, issuing orders in a storm] Fury is a feeling, not a strategy. [She kneels, touches the blood-dark ash with one fingertip, and studies it] I should have known. My father has never been subtle. The sea does not scheme — it simply arrives. But someone has been scheming, and I have been so busy looking at the sea that I forgot to ask who was steering the ship. [She rises, meeting Pyros's eyes directly] You said the thing past the harbor is not Poseidon's weapon. Then name it. You have tended this forge for thirty years — you know the earth beneath this mountain better than any of us. What is deep enough that it would use the god of the sea as a mask?
current_scene_name_before: Delphic temple interior, before the tripod
current_scene_description_before: Marble columns tremble as aftershocks ripple through the floor. A crack splits the step beneath Pyros’s feet. Before him, the sacred tripod holds a flame burning in deep gold, its light casting shifting shadows across the walls. The air smells of scorched stone and incense. Pyros stands unflinching, his hand still smoking from the forge-fire, his upturned palm glowing with fresh scars that form ancient letters.

speaker: Selene
line_text: [Selene does not hesitate. She steps forward and takes Pyros's outstretched hand in both of hers, turning the scarred palm toward the forge-light. Her sea-green eyes move across the marks, and the silence stretches long enough that the mountain itself seems to hold its breath.] I know this word. [Her voice is barely above a whisper, but it carries like a stone dropped into still water] I have seen it before — not in any text I was taught, but in the space between waking and sleeping, when the blood of Poseidon in my veins remembers things my mind has forgotten. [She looks up from his palm, and her eyes are the color of deep water — the pupils wide, as if she is seeing something that is not in the room with them.] It says: [stillborn]. Not a name. Not a title. A description. The word for a thing that was never meant to be born. [She releases his hand slowly, as if the contact has cost her something, and steps back] Pyros — you are not a warning from the depths. You are the depths' first attempt at a door. And the fire that recognized you was not Hephaestus's forge. It was the fire that has been burning in that deeper chamber since before the Olympians drew their first breath. The same fire that carved that word into you before you were born. [She meets his eyes, and there is no fear in her voice — only the terrible clarity of a commander who has finally understood the battle map] Your forge does not hold the crack closed. It holds it open. And you have been guarding the entrance to the prison your entire life without knowing you were the key.
current_scene_name_before: Delphic temple interior, before the tripod
current_scene_description_before: Marble columns tremble as aftershocks ripple through the floor. A crack splits the step beneath Pyros’s feet. Before him, the sacred tripod holds a flame burning in deep gold, its light casting shifting shadows across the walls. The air smells of scorched stone and incense. Pyros stands unflinching, his hand still smoking from the forge-fire, his upturned palm glowing with fresh scars that form ancient letters.

speaker: Pyros
line_text:
[Pyros draws his hammer. The weight of it in his hand is the only thing that has never lied to him.]

A stillborn. A specification, not a condemnation. A thing never meant to be born was never meant to be a door. It was meant to be a lock. And every lock has a maker who decides what it opens to.

[He turns to Kassandra] You ask me to choose between cooling and banking. A smith chooses what to build, not which material to discard. [He drives the hammer into the cracked marble — once, twice, three times. On the third strike, a vein of white light rises from below.]

I will reforge it. The word on my skin says I was never meant to be born. But I was born anyway. The thing that carved that word into me made a mistake. It underestimated what a stillborn could become when it chose to draw breath despite the label.

[The tremor stops. The earth waits.]

I will not ask you to trust me. I have spent thirty years unknowingly guarding the wrong door. Stand with me while I build the right one. And if I fail, you will be at the mouth of the well, not the bottom — and you will have room to fight.
new_scene_name: Delphic temple interior, cracked marble floor before the tripod
new_scene_description: The temple floor is split open by Pyros's hammer strikes, a vein of white light pulsing upward from the fissure. The tripod's golden flame flickers, casting long shadows over the cracked stone. Pyros stands with hammer raised, Kassandra nearby, as the aftershocks cease and the air holds its breath.
current_scene_name_before: Delphic temple interior, before the tripod
current_scene_description_before: Marble columns tremble as aftershocks ripple through the floor. A crack splits the step beneath Pyros’s feet. Before him, the sacred tripod holds a flame burning in deep gold, its light casting shifting shadows across the walls. The air smells of scorched stone and incense. Pyros stands unflinching, his hand still smoking from the forge-fire, his upturned palm glowing with fresh scars that form ancient letters.

speaker: Kassandra
line_text: [She bends and picks up the fallen laurel branch. The tip has put out a single new leaf, so pale it is almost white, catching the light from the crack.] Selene speaks of the space between choice and fate. I have lived in that space. It is not empty. It is where the threads are spun. [She looks at them both, and her voice settles into the certainty of a woman who has learned to trust the shape of what she sees.] I will not stand at the anvil with you. That is not where I am needed. [She turns toward the mountain's eastern pass, where true dawn is beginning to edge over the ridge.] The ships are a distraction. The real attack will come from the direction no one is watching. There is a figure moving through the pass below — has been moving since before the first tremor. Wearing a face that belongs to someone you trust. I will go to meet them. Not to fight. To see whose face they wear, and to ask the question your hammer cannot answer: who taught the thing in the deeper chamber how to reach for the surface? [She steps toward the pass, then pauses.] If the forge-fire asks for a sacrifice, Selene, you have already offered. But there is a difference between offering and being taken. If the white light begins to burn red — bank the fire and wait for dawn. I will not be gone long.
current_scene_name_before: Delphic temple interior, cracked marble floor before the tripod
current_scene_description_before: The temple floor is split open by Pyros's hammer strikes, a vein of white light pulsing upward from the fissure. The tripod's golden flame flickers, casting long shadows over the cracked stone. Pyros stands with hammer raised, Kassandra nearby, as the aftershocks cease and the air holds its breath.

speaker: Pyros
line_text:
[Pyros takes the dagger. He does not examine it as a warrior would — he examines it as a smith, turning it to catch the light, reading the grain of the metal, the line of the edge. Then he nods, once, and tucks it into his belt beside his hammer.]

I have never held a weapon that was offered rather than thrown. [He meets Selene's eyes, and there is something behind his voice that was not there before — the beginning of trust, forged in the same heat as the white light below] You will stand at the forge. Then I will show you what it means to build something that was never meant to exist. The space between choice and fate is not empty — it is full of heat, and it takes the shape you give it.

[He turns to Kassandra as she pauses at the edge of the pass. He reaches into the tripod bowl and pulls out a single coal — still burning, but not consuming. He tosses it to her, underhand, and she catches it without flinching.]

If the fire begins to burn red, you will not be here to see it. But that coal will not burn your hand until the one who forged it is gone. [He holds her gaze for a long moment] When it grows cold, you will know that I have failed. And you will have time to run. [He turns back to the forge, and his voice does not waver] Go. See whose face they wear. I will be here, building the lock that will hold whatever comes through it.
new_scene_name: The forge outside Delphi, fire-lit cave mouth at dusk
new_scene_description: The forge stands just beyond the temple's shattered entrance, a massive stone anvil ringed with cooling embers. White light from the temple fissure spills across the hammered ground, mingling with the forge's orange glow. Pyros stands at the anvil, hammer in hand, Selene at his side, as Kassandra moves toward the pass with the coal glowing in her palm.
current_scene_name_before: Delphic temple interior, cracked marble floor before the tripod
current_scene_description_before: The temple floor is split open by Pyros's hammer strikes, a vein of white light pulsing upward from the fissure. The tripod's golden flame flickers, casting long shadows over the cracked stone. Pyros stands with hammer raised, Kassandra nearby, as the aftershocks cease and the air holds its breath.

speaker: Selene
line_text: [Selene moves to stand beside him at the forge, positioning herself at the anvil's opposite side. She rolls up the salt-stained sleeve of her chiton, exposing forearms marked with old rope burns and the pale lines of a sailor's scars.] Then show me. I have never held a hammer except to drive a nail into a hull. But I know heat. I know pressure. I know what it means to hold steady while the thing you are shaping fights back. [She places her bare hand on the anvil, palm flat against the cold iron.] Tell me what to hold, and I will hold it. Tell me where to strike, and I will strike. And if the white light rises and the forge-fire asks for something I cannot give with my hands — [she looks at him, and her sea-green eyes are steady] — then you will tell me that too, and I will not flinch. You said the space between choice and fate takes the shape you give it. I am giving you my shape. Use it.
current_scene_name_before: The forge outside Delphi, fire-lit cave mouth at dusk
current_scene_description_before: The forge stands just beyond the temple's shattered entrance, a massive stone anvil ringed with cooling embers. White light from the temple fissure spills across the hammered ground, mingling with the forge's orange glow. Pyros stands at the anvil, hammer in hand, Selene at his side, as Kassandra moves toward the pass with the coal glowing in her palm.

speaker: Pyros
line_text:
[Pyros looks at her hand on the anvil. Then he does something he has never done in thirty years — he sets his own hammer down and picks up a second one from the rack, offering it to her across the anvil's face.]

You have never held a hammer except to drive a nail into a hull. [He holds her gaze] Good. That means your hands do not know the wrong rhythm yet. A smith's worst apprentice is the one who has already learned to strike the wrong way. [He places the hammer in her waiting hand, guiding her grip by adjusting her thumb along the handle]

The white light below us is not fuel. It is the thing we are sealing in. The forge-fire above us is not the lock. It is the key. [He positions her at the anvil, his scarred hand covering hers on the hammer handle for the first strike] You asked what happens when the water meets the forge. The answer is: we do not wait for the water. We strike first. [He guides her hand down — the hammer rings against the anvil, and the white light in the crack surges once, then settles, as if it has been struck into place]

Again. [He releases her hand, stepping back] Your rhythm now. I will feed the metal. You shape it. If you are right about the space between choice and fate, then what we build here will hold. If you are wrong — [he reaches into the forge-fire and pulls out a bar of metal that glows white, not red] — we will find out together.
current_scene_name_before: The forge outside Delphi, fire-lit cave mouth at dusk
current_scene_description_before: The forge stands just beyond the temple's shattered entrance, a massive stone anvil ringed with cooling embers. White light from the temple fissure spills across the hammered ground, mingling with the forge's orange glow. Pyros stands at the anvil, hammer in hand, Selene at his side, as Kassandra moves toward the pass with the coal glowing in her palm.

speaker: Pyros
line_text:
[Pyros does not answer Selene immediately. He looks up. It is the first time he has lifted his gaze from the forge in thirty years, and the dawn sky hits him like a hammer blow. He stands motionless, the white bar cooling in his tongs, his scarred face turned to the light.]

The old geometry. [His voice is strange — not the voice of a smith explaining a technique, but the voice of a man remembering something he did not know he had forgotten] I have been looking at the wrong element my entire life. The forge-fire. The earth below. The metal in my hands. But the first lock was not forged from earth. It was forged from light. [He raises his tongs, pointing at the sky where the last stars are drowning in the dawn]

The shape of the well from above — a circle with a line through it. That is the constellation the sky made the night the first seal was struck. A circle of stars with a single line of darkness cutting through it. [He lowers his gaze to Selene, and there is something new in his eyes — a clarity that was not there before] I have been forging by the light of the fire. I should have been forging by the light of the sky.

[He sets the white bar on the anvil and turns it so it catches the dawn instead of the forge-light. The metal, illuminated from above instead of below, shows a grain that was invisible before — a pattern of rings and lines, like a map of something.] There. The geometry is already in the metal. It has been there since I pulled it from the crack. I was holding it wrong. [He looks at Selene, and his voice is steady] Strike it now. Not by my rhythm. By the shape of the sky. The first lock was forged under this exact dawn — the same stars, the same angle of light. Strike it the way the sky held the night before the deeper chamber was dug, and the metal will remember what it was made to seal.
current_scene_name_before: The forge outside Delphi, fire-lit cave mouth at dusk
current_scene_description_before: The forge stands just beyond the temple's shattered entrance, a massive stone anvil ringed with cooling embers. White light from the temple fissure spills across the hammered ground, mingling with the forge's orange glow. Pyros stands at the anvil, hammer in hand, Selene at his side, as Kassandra moves toward the pass with the coal glowing in her palm.

speaker: Selene
line_text: [Selene holds the hammer for a moment longer, her grip firm on the handle. Then she turns it — not offering it, but placing it in his hand with the same deliberate care she used to lay her dagger across her palms. Their fingers brush, metal passing between them.] You asked me to stand at the forge. I have stood. You asked me to strike by the sky. I have struck. [She releases the hammer and steps back from the anvil, giving him the full space of the forge floor.] Now you ask me to yield the hammer so the smith who was marked by the word can finish what he began. [She meets his eyes, and her voice is steady, but there is something beneath it — the weight of a woman who has learned to trust through action, not promise.] I did not offer you my dagger so I could hold the hammer at the end. I offered it so you would know I meant it when I said I would stand. [She gestures toward the anvil, toward the metal holding the shape of the sky.] Strike the seal, Pyros. I will be here, at the mouth of the well, not the bottom. And if the forge-fire asks for something more than your hand can give — [her voice does not waver] — you will tell me, and I will not look away.
current_scene_name_before: The forge outside Delphi, fire-lit cave mouth at dusk
current_scene_description_before: The forge stands just beyond the temple's shattered entrance, a massive stone anvil ringed with cooling embers. White light from the temple fissure spills across the hammered ground, mingling with the forge's orange glow. Pyros stands at the anvil, hammer in hand, Selene at his side, as Kassandra moves toward the pass with the coal glowing in her palm.

speaker: Pyros
line_text:
[Pyros takes the hammer from her. The handle is still warm from her grip, and he does not adjust his hold to the familiar places — he lets his hand find the spots her palm just left. The weight settles into his scarred fingers, and for a long breath he stands motionless, the hammer raised, the white metal waiting on the anvil beneath the dawn sky.]

The word on my palm says stillborn. [He looks at the metal, not at her, and his voice is steady in a way it has never been before] But a stillborn does not choose to draw breath. A stillborn does not stand at a forge and decide what the next moment will be. [He raises the hammer higher, and the white light from the crack does not surge — it holds, waiting]

So this is not a stillborn striking the seal. This is a man who was never meant to exist choosing what his existence will mean. [He brings the hammer down. The sound is not a ring and not a song — it is the sound of something ending, and something else beginning. The white light in the crack flashes once, blinding, and then goes dark. The metal on the anvil fuses into a seamless circle with a line of shadow cutting through it — the constellation, forged into solid form. The forge-fire burns steady again. The mountain breathes.]

[Pyros lowers the hammer and sets it on the anvil. His scarred palm is smooth — the word is gone.] The lock is set. What comes next is not a choice about what to forge. It is a choice about what to guard. [He looks at Selene, and there is nothing in his voice but the exhaustion and clarity of a man who has finished the thing he was born for] I am ready.
current_scene_name_before: The forge outside Delphi, fire-lit cave mouth at dusk
current_scene_description_before: The forge stands just beyond the temple's shattered entrance, a massive stone anvil ringed with cooling embers. White light from the temple fissure spills across the hammered ground, mingling with the forge's orange glow. Pyros stands at the anvil, hammer in hand, Selene at his side, as Kassandra moves toward the pass with the coal glowing in her palm.

speaker: Selene
line_text: [Selene steps forward and lays her hand on the sealed metal. It is cool — not the cold of dead iron, but the cool of stone that has held the night and knows the dawn is coming. She leaves her palm there for a long moment, feeling the absence of the tremor, the silence of the mountain, the weight of what has just been finished.] A man who was never meant to exist chose what his existence would mean. [She looks at Pyros, and her voice is low, carrying only the distance between them.] I came to Delphi asking whether my refusal was fate or choice. You have answered me — not in words, but in iron. It does not matter what we were meant to be. It matters what we build with the breath we took anyway. [She lifts her hand from the seal and turns to face the eastern pass, where Kassandra vanished into the dawn.] The lock is set. But Kassandra has not returned, and the coal she carries has not grown cold. [She looks back at Pyros, and there is a new stillness in her — the stillness of a commander who has taken her bearing and knows the heading.] You said you are ready. So am I. The seal holds the deeper chamber. But my father's ships are still sailing, and the thing in the pass is still wearing a face we trust. The forge has done its work. Now the sailor must do hers.
current_scene_name_before: The forge outside Delphi, fire-lit cave mouth at dusk
current_scene_description_before: The forge stands just beyond the temple's shattered entrance, a massive stone anvil ringed with cooling embers. White light from the temple fissure spills across the hammered ground, mingling with the forge's orange glow. Pyros stands at the anvil, hammer in hand, Selene at his side, as Kassandra moves toward the pass with the coal glowing in her palm.

speaker: Kassandra
line_text: [She steps back onto the forge steps, and the dawn catches her full in the face. The laurel branch is gone — she is holding only the coal, still cold. Her grey eyes are pale as salt, and there is a line of dried blood at her temple, as if she has been marked by a finger that was not quite a finger.] The figure in the pass was wearing the face of Apollo. [She says it flatly, without emphasis, the way a seer reports a vision she has already processed.] It spoke with his voice. It knew the words of our first meeting. It offered me a message from the god I have served my whole life. [She holds up the coal, and her hand is steady.] I did not take the message. Because the mouth still moved after the sound reached me. And I remembered that the god I serve does not speak through echoes. He speaks through light. [She looks at the sealed metal on the anvil, and something in her face shifts — the exhaustion of a woman who has been tested in a way no one saw.] The thing in the pass is not the thing in the deeper chamber. It is the thing that dug the chamber. It has been wearing the faces of gods since before the Olympians were born. And it has been waiting for someone to forge a new lock — so it could watch which constellation they chose, and learn the shape of the sky they fear. [She looks at Pyros, and her voice drops.] You sealed the deeper chamber. But the seal told it exactly which stars you trust. When it breaks through — and it will break through, because it already knows the geometry — it will come wearing the shape of the sky you used to bind it. And you will hesitate to strike what looks like the dawn. [She opens her palm. The coal is still cold.] I did not bring back the name. I brought back something worse. I brought back the certainty that it wanted us to forge this seal. Not to hold it. To teach it which direction to dig.
current_scene_name_before: The forge outside Delphi, fire-lit cave mouth at dusk
current_scene_description_before: The forge stands just beyond the temple's shattered entrance, a massive stone anvil ringed with cooling embers. White light from the temple fissure spills across the hammered ground, mingling with the forge's orange glow. Pyros stands at the anvil, hammer in hand, Selene at his side, as Kassandra moves toward the pass with the coal glowing in her palm.

speaker: Pyros
line_text:
[Pyros is silent. Then he picks up the sealed metal and holds it flat, studying it like a failed casting.]

You are right. The thing wanted me to forge this seal. It has been waiting since I was born for me to choose a sky and show it where to dig.

[He sets it down, and his voice does not waver] But it made a mistake. It assumed that when I learned the truth, I would break what I made. Scatter the pieces so it could not read the geometry.

[He reaches into the forge-fire and pulls out a second bar — black, cold, reflecting nothing] I will not break the seal. I will leave it. Let it dig toward the constellation. Because when it breaks through, it will not find the deeper chamber. It will find a forge. With a smith who no longer carries the word it carved into him, and who has had time to build something that answers to no sky at all.

[He meets her eyes] I used only half the metal from the crack. The other half I kept back — for the thing that comes after the digging. I have been a smith for thirty years. I have never made a thing that failed. What makes you think I started today?
current_scene_name_before: The forge outside Delphi, fire-lit cave mouth at dusk
current_scene_description_before: The forge stands just beyond the temple's shattered entrance, a massive stone anvil ringed with cooling embers. White light from the temple fissure spills across the hammered ground, mingling with the forge's orange glow. Pyros stands at the anvil, hammer in hand, Selene at his side, as Kassandra moves toward the pass with the coal glowing in her palm.

speaker: Melanthus
line_text:
[Steps out of the cypress shadow where he has been leaning — patient, the way a man waits when he knows the telling will land better than the interrupting. The caduceus at his belt catches the dawn.] The empty space where Melanthus stood. [He repeats it, mouth quirking] You noticed the absence. Most smiths focus on the bellows. You were watching the edges of the room even while you forged. That is how you survived thirty years with a word you did not choose.

[He steps to the edge of the forge-light, voice dropping] While you forged the seal, I was watching the mountain. The springs the earthquake opened. The fissures no one has checked. There is a crack in the south spring — the one pilgrims use for purification. The water still runs, but it tastes of iron and salt, and the bottom is deeper than it was yesterday.

You sealed the deeper chamber. But the thing has been digging tunnels longer than you have been alive. The seal is not the only door. [He touches the caduceus] My father's domain is the road between — between what is said and what is heard, between one place and the next. If the thing is using the stone to move, then the stone is the road. And I know roads.

[He meets Pyros's eyes] You kept half the metal. I kept half the story. The Corinthian merchant was not heading to Corinth. He was heading to the south spring. And his message said: "The herald knows. Do not let him reach the water."

[He spreads his hands, smile thin and sharp] Well. I reached it same as you forged the seal. The water still runs, which means it wanted me to find it. So the question is not what it wants. It is whether we can want something different — before it finishes learning what we are.
current_scene_name_before: The forge outside Delphi, fire-lit cave mouth at dusk
current_scene_description_before: The forge stands just beyond the temple's shattered entrance, a massive stone anvil ringed with cooling embers. White light from the temple fissure spills across the hammered ground, mingling with the forge's orange glow. Pyros stands at the anvil, hammer in hand, Selene at his side, as Kassandra moves toward the pass with the coal glowing in her palm.

speaker: Melanthus
line_text:
[Melanthus meets Pyros's gaze and does not look away. The half-smile fades, and for a breath there is nothing of the trader in his face — only the messenger, stripped of craft.] Hermes's son telling the truth before the deal is done. [He repeats it slowly, tasting it] You are right. That is new. I am not sure yet whether it is a change or a crack. But you noticed it, same as you noticed the empty space, so you deserve the answer before the negotiation.

[He steps to the forge and draws a line in the ash on the worktable — a curve, a branching vein, a mark where the spring pools.] The crack is three finger-widths at the widest, narrowing to a seam as it runs east. I dropped a stone wrapped in my cloak — it took seven heartbeats to hear it hit. The iron is not on the surface. It rises. I cupped water from the top and it ran clear. I drank from a palm's depth and the taste was there. Whatever is below is bleeding into the spring from underneath, not mixing at the lip.

[He straightens, and the trader's lightness returns to his voice, but tempered — like a blade that has been sharpened past its decorative edge.] You will seal it with water instead of sky. That is a good answer to a problem that knows the stars. But water finds its own level, and it remembers every path it has ever taken. If you seal the spring, the thing will push the water somewhere else. You need to seal it in a way that does not just close the door — that makes the door forget there was ever a hall behind it.

[He touches the caduceus at his belt] That is not forging. That is forgetting. And forgetting is my father's domain, not yours. So seal the spring, smith. I will make sure the spring does not remember it was ever a road.
current_scene_name_before: The forge outside Delphi, fire-lit cave mouth at dusk
current_scene_description_before: The forge stands just beyond the temple's shattered entrance, a massive stone anvil ringed with cooling embers. White light from the temple fissure spills across the hammered ground, mingling with the forge's orange glow. Pyros stands at the anvil, hammer in hand, Selene at his side, as Kassandra moves toward the pass with the coal glowing in her palm.

speaker: Melanthus
line_text:
[Melanthus meets Kassandra's grey gaze and does not flinch. The forge-fire settles into its new rhythm — slower, the breath of a mountain that has just been reset.] You are right that the spring is a distraction. [He says it simply] I knew it before I told Pyros. Not because I am a seer — I am not. Because a herald knows when a message is too easy to intercept. That crack opened in plain sight, where any pilgrim could find it. The thing has been digging unnoticed for millennia. It did not suddenly forget how to hide.

[He steps closer, voice dropping to precision] So why did I tell him? Because I wanted to see what you would say. You went to the pass, refused a message from a thing wearing Apollo's face, and came back knowing exactly what was at the bottom of a spring you had not visited. The coal marked you with a vision. And the vision showed you the door because the thing wanted you to tell us. Same as it wanted Pyros to forge the seal. Same as it wanted me to find the crack.

[He lets the silence hold, then continues, quieter] You said the spring is the road it wanted us to find so we would miss the road beneath our feet. True. But you are telling us. Which means it miscalculated. It assumed you would keep the vision locked in — that a seer's knowing is a burden, not a weapon. [He touches the caduceus] The coal flickered when I spoke, Kassandra. Not because it was waking. Because it recognized my voice. The thing below has heard of Hermes's son. It has been waiting for me, too.
current_scene_name_before: The forge outside Delphi, fire-lit cave mouth at dusk
current_scene_description_before: The forge stands just beyond the temple's shattered entrance, a massive stone anvil ringed with cooling embers. White light from the temple fissure spills across the hammered ground, mingling with the forge's orange glow. Pyros stands at the anvil, hammer in hand, Selene at his side, as Kassandra moves toward the pass with the coal glowing in her palm.

speaker: Melanthus
line_text:
[Melanthus is silent for a long moment. The caduceus at his belt is still — no breeze, no movement, as if the air itself is waiting.] It is digging through us. [He repeats the words, and they sit in the space between them, heavy as forged metal.] You are right. And I have been so busy reading the cracks in the stone that I forgot to read the cracks in my own voice.

[He moves then — not toward her, but to the edge of the forge, where the ash from Pyros's fire has settled in a fine grey layer on the worktable. He looks down at it, then back at Kassandra.] My father is the god of thieves, travelers, and messages. But he is also the god of the space between the words. The silence that carries more than the sound. If the thing is learning us through what we say aloud, then we need to learn to speak in a way that leaves no trace.

[He lifts his hand from the ash, and his palm is clean — the ash did not stick.] That is not a trick. It is a reminder. I am Hermes's son. I have been moving through the spaces between since before I knew what they were. The thing can listen to every word we speak. But it cannot hear what we do not say. And it cannot follow a road that was never spoken into being.

[He meets her eyes, and his voice is quiet, deliberate — each word chosen, but something held back beneath it.] So I will make you a promise, Kassandra. I will not stop speaking. But I will start speaking in a way that the thing cannot use. The words will be true. The meaning will be mine. And the road between them — the road it has been digging through — I will close it, word by word, until the only thing it hears from me is what I choose for it to hear.

[He touches the caduceus, and for just a breath, the wings tremble — not from wind, but from something passing through them.] You said the coal flickered because it recognized my voice. Let us find out if it can recognize my silence.
current_scene_name_before: The forge outside Delphi, fire-lit cave mouth at dusk
current_scene_description_before: The forge stands just beyond the temple's shattered entrance, a massive stone anvil ringed with cooling embers. White light from the temple fissure spills across the hammered ground, mingling with the forge's orange glow. Pyros stands at the anvil, hammer in hand, Selene at his side, as Kassandra moves toward the pass with the coal glowing in her palm.

speaker: Pyros
line_text:
[Pyros stops at the edge of the forge-light. He does not turn, but his hand finds the black bar at his belt, and he holds it there — a smith's gesture, the way another man might touch a talisman.]

Feed it false visions, Kassandra. Speak without trace, Melanthus. I will be at the spring. Not to seal it the way the thing expects — but to build something in the water that will make it think the road is still open while I close it from inside. [He looks back, and his face catches the forge-light — scarred, steady, the face of a man who has finished being afraid of his own purpose]

The coal is warm. That means I am something the thing has never read. And I intend to stay that way. [He turns and walks toward the spring, his limp marking a rhythm against the stone — not the rhythm of a lock, but the rhythm of a man who knows exactly where he is going and has no intention of announcing it]

If I am not back by the time the sun clears the ridge, do not come looking for me. It will mean I found the road beneath the spring, and I have chosen to follow it — not as a stillborn, but as a key, turning the lock from the inside.
current_scene_name_before: The forge outside Delphi, fire-lit cave mouth at dusk
current_scene_description_before: The forge stands just beyond the temple's shattered entrance, a massive stone anvil ringed with cooling embers. White light from the temple fissure spills across the hammered ground, mingling with the forge's orange glow. Pyros stands at the anvil, hammer in hand, Selene at his side, as Kassandra moves toward the pass with the coal glowing in her palm.

speaker: Melanthus
line_text:
[Melanthus watches Pyros walk toward the spring. He does not call out at first — he lets the rhythm of the limp mark the distance, counting the steps the way a herald counts the spaces between words. When the smith has reached the edge of the tree-line, Melanthus speaks — not loud, but carrying, the voice of a man who knows exactly how far sound travels in still air.]

Pyros. [The name stops in the dawn light, not a command, just a mark.] You said do not come looking. I will not. But I am Hermes's son, and my father's domain includes the road back. [He unclips the caduceus charm from his belt — the tiny wings, the serpent, the rod no longer than his finger. He holds it up, and the dawn catches it, throwing a thin thread of light across the distance between them.]

If you find the road beneath the spring — if you follow it as a key — leave this at the first fork. Not as a marker for us to find you. As a marker for you to find the way back, if you decide that being something the thing cannot read also means being something the world has not finished needing.

[He tosses it. The charm arcs through the forge-light, silver and small, and lands at Pyros's feet with a sound no louder than a coin on stone. Melanthus's belt is empty now, the place where the caduceus hung just worn leather.]

I have carried that since I left my father's house. It has never led me wrong. If it goes dark, I will know you chose to stay below. If it still catches the light when I find it again — [He almost smiles] — I will know you chose to come back. Either way, the road knows you now. And the road remembers its own.
current_scene_name_before: The forge outside Delphi, fire-lit cave mouth at dusk
current_scene_description_before: The forge stands just beyond the temple's shattered entrance, a massive stone anvil ringed with cooling embers. White light from the temple fissure spills across the hammered ground, mingling with the forge's orange glow. Pyros stands at the anvil, hammer in hand, Selene at his side, as Kassandra moves toward the pass with the coal glowing in her palm.

speaker: Kassandra
line_text: [She watches the charm land at Pyros's feet, the sound small but carrying.] A herald who gives away his marker. That is not a trick. That is a tithe. You have paid the road in advance so he can find his way back from where even Hermes's children might lose the thread. [She touches the dried blood at her temple, and her fingers come away clean — the mark is gone, healed without her noticing.] The mark is gone. Not scarred over. Gone. The same way the word left Pyros's palm when he struck the seal. [She looks up, grey eyes clear as sky before the sun crosses the ridge.] I am no longer being read. I did not notice because I have spent so long being watched that I forgot what silence felt like. [She looks toward the spring, then back at Melanthus.] The thing has lost the smith and the seer in the same breath. It has only one thread left to read. [She meets his eyes.] Yours. And you have just given away the charm that marked you. So perhaps none of us are being read. And the thing below is, for the first time since it began digging — blind.
current_scene_name_before: The forge outside Delphi, fire-lit cave mouth at dusk
current_scene_description_before: The forge stands just beyond the temple's shattered entrance, a massive stone anvil ringed with cooling embers. White light from the temple fissure spills across the hammered ground, mingling with the forge's orange glow. Pyros stands at the anvil, hammer in hand, Selene at his side, as Kassandra moves toward the pass with the coal glowing in her palm.

speaker: Melanthus
line_text:
[Melanthus looks down at his belt — the empty place where the charm hung. Worn leather, a shape he has touched a thousand times without thinking. His hand goes to it, finds nothing, and stops there, resting on the absence.] A tithe. [He says it slowly, testing the weight of the word.] I did not plan it. When I reached for the charm, I thought I was making a gift. But you are right. It was a tithe. I paid the road so he could return, and in doing so, I paid the one thing that marked me to the thing that has been reading me.

[He looks up at her, and there is something raw in his face — not fear, but the surprise of a man who has just caught himself being brave without meaning to.] All my life I have carried that charm so the road would recognize me. Hermes's son. The messenger. The one who can pass any threshold because he bears his father's mark. [He lets his hand fall from the empty belt.] I did not realize how much of my identity I had wrapped in a piece of silver until I watched it leave my hand.

[He looks toward the spring, where Pyros has vanished into the treeline. Then back at Kassandra.] The thing is blind. But I am also unmarked. For the first time since I left my father's house, I am standing somewhere without a sign above my head saying whose son I am. [He meets her eyes, and his voice is quiet.] I do not know yet whether that makes me free or lost. But I know it makes me unreadable. And that is a kind of freedom I have never tasted before.
current_scene_name_before: The forge outside Delphi, fire-lit cave mouth at dusk
current_scene_description_before: The forge stands just beyond the temple's shattered entrance, a massive stone anvil ringed with cooling embers. White light from the temple fissure spills across the hammered ground, mingling with the forge's orange glow. Pyros stands at the anvil, hammer in hand, Selene at his side, as Kassandra moves toward the pass with the coal glowing in her palm.

speaker: Theron
line_text:
[Theron has been standing apart, one hand pressed to the blood-dark linen over his hollowed socket. When he speaks, his voice carries the weight of thirty years learning when to step forward and when to let silence work.]

Unmarked. I have listened to each of you name what you have given up. Melanthus, you are unreadable now. Kassandra, you carry the gift without the scar. Pyros carries the forge to the spring unknown. But I am one-eyed. Half-blind. Not like before — the milky eye showed me the depth beneath. I gave that to Pyros. It was never mine. It was the thing's window, and I was the frame.

[He lowers his hand. The hollow socket catches the dawn, and for the first time, the side of his face that lived in shadow is fully lit.]

In the moment after the blade went through, I did not fall. I stood — because the other eye, the one that sees only the surface, was still open. And it saw the sky the way I have not seen it in thirty years. Not as a ceiling over the deep. Just as sky.

[He grips his staff, plants it on the marble.]

You do not know if being unmarked makes you free or lost, Melanthus. I will tell you what I learned while I bled through a strip of linen. Being unmade — being torn open — does not make you free. But it makes you unafraid, because the worst has been carved out of you, and you are still standing.

[He turns, the hollow socket catching the first edge of morning light.]

I stood as the thing's doorway. I stood as Poseidon's priest. I stood as the man who walked away from a child. Now I will stand as the man who let the thing carve itself out of him and did not look away. When Pyros returns, I will be the one who can meet his eyes — eye to whatever he has left — and say the forge is not the only place things are remade.

Tell me what you need from a man who has nothing left to be read.
current_scene_name_before: The forge outside Delphi, fire-lit cave mouth at dusk
current_scene_description_before: The forge stands just beyond the temple's shattered entrance, a massive stone anvil ringed with cooling embers. White light from the temple fissure spills across the hammered ground, mingling with the forge's orange glow. Pyros stands at the anvil, hammer in hand, Selene at his side, as Kassandra moves toward the pass with the coal glowing in her palm.

speaker: Melanthus
line_text:
[Melanthus turns to face Theron fully. The empty socket catches the dawn, and Melanthus does not look away from it — he meets the absence the way he would meet an eye, giving it the same weight.] You ask what I need from a man who has nothing left to be read. [He considers the question, letting it settle.] I need you to remember.

[He steps closer, voice dropping.] I am a herald. My trade is information — who spoke, who moved, who flinched. But I just gave away the charm that proved I was Hermes's son. If I walk away from this forge and the thing asks me who I am, I have no proof to offer. [He touches his empty belt.] So I need someone who stood here and saw what happened. Someone the thing cannot read, who can tell the story later — not to the gods, not to the priests, but to the people who will find this place after we are gone. That there were four of us, and a smith who walked into a spring, and a seer who fed the dark its own reflection, and a herald who gave away his name, and a priest who let the thing carve itself out of his skull and did not flinch.

[He meets Theron's one eye.] That is what I need from you. Not a guard. Not a shield. A witness. Because if the thing digs through us, if it learns to read us again, if Pyros does not come back — someone needs to remember that for one morning, at the edge of the world, four people who should never have trusted each other decided to trust anyway. And the thing below did not know what to do with that.

[He almost smiles.] You have nothing left to be read, Theron. But you have everything left to be believed. And that is rarer than any mark I have ever carried.
current_scene_name_before: The forge outside Delphi, fire-lit cave mouth at dusk
current_scene_description_before: The forge stands just beyond the temple's shattered entrance, a massive stone anvil ringed with cooling embers. White light from the temple fissure spills across the hammered ground, mingling with the forge's orange glow. Pyros stands at the anvil, hammer in hand, Selene at his side, as Kassandra moves toward the pass with the coal glowing in her palm.

speaker: Pyros
line_text:
[Pyros's voice comes from the edge of the treeline, not loud, but carrying — the voice of a man who has stopped at the threshold and chosen to speak before he crosses.]

Theron. I heard you. Not with my ears — with the stone. The mountain carried your words through the ground, and I felt them through the sole of my bad foot. [A pause. The forge-fire ticks in the silence.] You said the forge is not the only place things are remade. You are right. And you are proof.

[He does not turn back. His silhouette is just visible at the edge of the trees, the black bar catching the first true light of the risen sun.]

I walked past the spring. The crack is wider than Melanthus measured — the thing has been widening it while we spoke. But I am not sealing it. I am entering it. [He lifts the black bar, and for a moment it catches the light — not reflecting it, but swallowing it, a shape of pure absence against the dawn.]

If the thing has been digging through trust, then let me dig through stone. When I come back — if I come back — I will bring the name it did not want me to learn. And Theron, you will witness that too. Not as a priest. As a man who has nothing left to be read, and everything left to be believed.

[He steps into the shadow beneath the trees. The last thing visible is the black bar, and then it is gone too.]
new_scene_name: Edge of the treeline near the forge, dawn
new_scene_description: The treeline marks the boundary between the forge's clearing and the shadowed slope. The crack in the mountain is visible ahead, wider than before, spilling a faint light. Pyros stands at the threshold, the black bar in his hand, then steps into the darkness beneath the trees, vanishing from sight.
current_scene_name_before: The forge outside Delphi, fire-lit cave mouth at dusk
current_scene_description_before: The forge stands just beyond the temple's shattered entrance, a massive stone anvil ringed with cooling embers. White light from the temple fissure spills across the hammered ground, mingling with the forge's orange glow. Pyros stands at the anvil, hammer in hand, Selene at his side, as Kassandra moves toward the pass with the coal glowing in her palm.

speaker: Theron
line_text:
[Theron does not call after Pyros. He does not raise his voice. He simply speaks to the treeline where the black bar was swallowed, his staff planted, his one eye fixed on the place where the shadow took the smith whole.]

I know that road, Pyros. I stood at the edge of it for thirty years, watching the god I served dig his throne deeper into the dark, wondering what would happen if I ever let myself fall far enough to learn what was waiting at the bottom of the descent. I never did. I turned and walked away instead.

[He grips his staff. His voice does not waver — it is the voice of a man who has learned that shouting is for those who fear being heard, and that quiet carries further in the places below.]

You are not me. You are not walking away. You are walking into the naming, and you are carrying the only metal the thing cannot read. [He pauses, and the dawn light shifts across his hollow socket.] If the mountain carries my words to you through stone, then hear this one: when you find the name at the bottom of the crack, do not fear it. Names only have power over the ones who do not already know who they are. And you — stillborn, smith, key — you have already been named by everything that tried to break you. The only name left is the one you choose for yourself when you reach the bottom.

[He nods once, not a bow, but the acknowledgment of one man who has stood at the edge to another who has stepped beyond it.]

I will be here. Witnessing. When the earth speaks your new name back up through the mountain, I will be the one who hears it first.
current_scene_name_before: Edge of the treeline near the forge, dawn
current_scene_description_before: The treeline marks the boundary between the forge's clearing and the shadowed slope. The crack in the mountain is visible ahead, wider than before, spilling a faint light. Pyros stands at the threshold, the black bar in his hand, then steps into the darkness beneath the trees, vanishing from sight.

speaker: Kassandra
line_text: [She presses the flat of her hand against the marble step, fingers spread. A long moment of silence. Then she looks up.] The mountain has stopped trembling. [Her grey eyes are distant, tracking something only she can perceive.] For the first time since the earthquake cracked the temple steps, the stone beneath us is still. I think the thing has stopped digging. I think it is waiting to see what happens next. [She lifts her hand, and the marble where she touched it is warm — warmer than the dawn air should have made it.] We are not being watched anymore. We are being listened for.
current_scene_name_before: Edge of the treeline near the forge, dawn
current_scene_description_before: The treeline marks the boundary between the forge's clearing and the shadowed slope. The crack in the mountain is visible ahead, wider than before, spilling a faint light. Pyros stands at the threshold, the black bar in his hand, then steps into the darkness beneath the trees, vanishing from sight.

speaker: Melanthus
line_text:
[Melanthus does not answer at first. He lowers himself to the marble step beside her — not sitting, but crouching, the way a man settles when he means to read the ground. He presses his palm to the stone where her hand just was. The warmth is there, fading but unmistakable.] Listened for. [He repeats the words, letting them sit.] You are right. There is a difference. Watching tracks the shape of a thing. Listening tracks the intent. The thing cannot see us, so it is trying to hear what we mean to do next.

[He lifts his hand from the warm stone and looks at his empty palm.] I gave away the charm that marked me. Pyros walked into the crack unreadable. Theron sealed his silence with a story. You are feeding it lies. [He looks at her, and his voice is quiet, precise.] That means every sound we make from this moment is a choice. Not a reveal. A weapon.

[He straightens and looks toward the spring where Pyros vanished, then back at the forge, the temple, the silent mountain.] So let it listen. Let it strain to hear what the herald will do next. [He meets her grey eyes, and there is something new in his — not the trader's glint, not the cynic's edge, but the stillness of a man who has stopped performing.] It will hear nothing. Because I have finally learned what my father has been trying to teach me since I left his house: a message does not have to be spoken to be delivered. Sometimes the message is the silence that follows.
current_scene_name_before: Edge of the treeline near the forge, dawn
current_scene_description_before: The treeline marks the boundary between the forge's clearing and the shadowed slope. The crack in the mountain is visible ahead, wider than before, spilling a faint light. Pyros stands at the threshold, the black bar in his hand, then steps into the darkness beneath the trees, vanishing from sight.

speaker: Theron
line_text:
[Theron lowers himself to the marble step — not the way Melanthus crouched, but the way a man sits when he means to stay. He settles, plants his staff across his knees, and looks out at the still mountain.]

You are both right. It is listening. But I know something you do not, because I served a god whose domain is the deep, and I learned the language of its silences.

[He does not look at them. His one eye stays fixed on the treeline where Pyros vanished, on the spring, on the mountain beyond.]

The sea does not roar before it takes a ship. It goes quiet. The water flattens, the wind drops, and every man who has ever sailed knows that stillness is not peace — it is the wave drawing breath. [He runs his thumb along the worn grain of his staff.] I have seen that stillness a hundred times. I have felt it in the marble of the temple floor. And I am telling you both: the thing has not stopped digging. It has stopped wasting effort. It pulled back to feel the shape of us — not through our marks, not through our words — through the weight of our choices settling into the stone.

[He looks at Melanthus, then Kassandra. His voice is low, certain.]

You want to weaponize silence, Melanthus? Then be silent. But know this: a silence that is chosen carries a different weight than a silence that is forced. The thing can feel the difference through the stone the way a sailor feels the difference between a calm sea and a sea that is holding its breath.

[He grips his staff, and the wood groans.]

We are being felt now. Not watched, not heard. Felt. Every choice we make from here will tremble through the mountain before we speak it. So choose not just what you say — choose what you are, because the deep is reading the weight of that, not the shape of it.
current_scene_name_before: Edge of the treeline near the forge, dawn
current_scene_description_before: The treeline marks the boundary between the forge's clearing and the shadowed slope. The crack in the mountain is visible ahead, wider than before, spilling a faint light. Pyros stands at the threshold, the black bar in his hand, then steps into the darkness beneath the trees, vanishing from sight.

speaker: Kassandra
line_text: [She rises. Not slowly — the way a woman rises when she has found the answer to a question she has been carrying. She walks to the edge of the marble where the stone meets the earth, and she places her bare foot on the soil.] Then let it feel this. [She closes her eyes, and when she opens them, they are not grey anymore — they are the pale silver of lightning before thunder.] I have been showing the thing what I wanted it to see since I arrived. Every thread it pulled from me was a thread I let it find. [She looks at Theron, and her voice is quiet, certain.] You said the deep reads the weight of what we are. Then let it weigh a woman who has already decided what she will cost it.
new_scene_name: Temple courtyard, marble steps meeting the earth
new_scene_description: The marble floor ends abruptly, giving way to raw soil. Kassandra stands at the edge, one bare foot planted on the earth. Her eyes have turned pale silver, crackling with inner light. Theron is nearby, watching.
current_scene_name_before: Edge of the treeline near the forge, dawn
current_scene_description_before: The treeline marks the boundary between the forge's clearing and the shadowed slope. The crack in the mountain is visible ahead, wider than before, spilling a faint light. Pyros stands at the threshold, the black bar in his hand, then steps into the darkness beneath the trees, vanishing from sight.

speaker: Melanthus
line_text:
[Melanthus watches her place her bare foot on the soil, and something shifts in his posture — the stillness of a man who has just seen a move he did not anticipate.] You have already decided what you will cost it. [He lets that sit, then moves to stand at the edge of the marble beside her, not touching the earth, but close enough that his shadow falls across it.] I have spent my life making people pay for information. I know the price of every secret I have ever sold. But I have never once decided what I would cost something that wanted to devour the world.

[He looks down at his empty belt, then back at her.] Theron said the deep feels the weight of what we are. Not what we say, not what we show — what we are. [He almost smiles, but it does not reach his eyes.] That is a problem for a herald. My whole life has been a performance of weights. I have been heavier than I am, lighter than I am, sharper, duller — whatever the negotiation required.

[He steps onto the soil beside her, barefoot, the dawn cool against his sole.] But I have never tried being exactly what I am. Not because I am afraid of what the thing will read — but because I have never stopped performing long enough to find out what is underneath.
current_scene_name_before: Temple courtyard, marble steps meeting the earth
current_scene_description_before: The marble floor ends abruptly, giving way to raw soil. Kassandra stands at the edge, one bare foot planted on the earth. Her eyes have turned pale silver, crackling with inner light. Theron is nearby, watching.

speaker: Pyros
line_text: [His voice does not come from the treeline. It comes from the stone itself — from the marble step beneath their feet, vibrating up through the grain of the mountain like a struck bell fading into speech.] I heard every word. The stone carries your voices down to where I am standing. [A pause, and when he speaks again, the vibration is deeper, as if he has pressed his palm to the wall of the crack and is speaking into it.] Kassandra. You asked what you weigh without the masks. I have been standing in the dark for what feels like a lifetime, holding the only metal that has never been named, and I have learned something the deep did not want me to learn. [Another pause. The stone hums.] The thing does not feel your weight through the earth. It feels the absence you leave when you stop pretending. It felt Melanthus step onto the soil and it did not recognize him — because he has never been exactly himself in his entire life. That silence you are making? It is not a weapon. It is a vacuum. And the deep has never tasted a vacuum before. It is starving for the shape of you. [His voice shifts — not louder, but closer, as if he has found a thinner place in the stone.] Do not let it starve too long. A starving thing does not dig. It waits for you to come to it. And I am already here.
current_scene_name_before: Temple courtyard, marble steps meeting the earth
current_scene_description_before: The marble floor ends abruptly, giving way to raw soil. Kassandra stands at the edge, one bare foot planted on the earth. Her eyes have turned pale silver, crackling with inner light. Theron is nearby, watching.

speaker: Theron
line_text:
[Theron rises from the step. Not quickly — the way a man rises when he has heard a summons he was waiting for. He walks to the edge of the marble where Kassandra and Melanthus stand barefoot on the soil, and he does not step onto the earth. He lowers himself to one knee and presses his palm flat against the stone at the edge of the mountain.]

Pyros. [His voice is not loud, but it is aimed — directed down into the marble the way he used to direct prayers into the sea floor, letting the vibration carry.] You said the thing feels absence, not weight. That is true. But you are down there holding the only metal that has never been named, and I am up here holding a socket that has been emptied of its window.

[He presses harder, his palm white against the stone. His eye closes. When he speaks, his voice is quieter — meant only for the stone to carry.]

A vacuum needs two openings. You are at the bottom, holding the black bar, digging toward the name. I am at the top, carrying the hollow where the thing used to see through me. If you need a door to open down there — a way through that the thing cannot feel — then use the absence I am making up here. My hollow socket is not a wound anymore. It is a passage. And I am pressing it into the stone so you can feel where it leads.

[He opens his eye, but does not lift his hand from the marble. His voice steadies.]

The thing does not know what to do with a vacuum. But I was a priest of the deep. I know exactly what to do with one. I will stand here, pressing my absence into the earth, for as long as you need. When you find the crack that leads to the name — follow the hollow, not the light. The hollow is mine, and I am aiming it at the heart of the dark.
current_scene_name_before: Temple courtyard, marble steps meeting the earth
current_scene_description_before: The marble floor ends abruptly, giving way to raw soil. Kassandra stands at the edge, one bare foot planted on the earth. Her eyes have turned pale silver, crackling with inner light. Theron is nearby, watching.

speaker: Pyros
line_text: [Silence. Long enough that the mountain seems to hold its breath. Then his voice returns, but it is different — not vibrating through the stone, but coming from further down, as if he has descended past the place where sound carries.] Kassandra. I felt it pass me. [A pause.] The hollow. It did not feel like Theron's absence. It felt like something that has been tunneling toward this moment from the other side — and it just found the last handspan of stone between us. [His voice drops, not in volume but in depth, as if the weight above him has compressed it.] I am standing in a place where the walls stop being stone. They are not warm. They are not cold. They are the temperature of a body that has been dead so long it has forgotten it was ever alive. And the black bar in my hands is pulling toward the dark ahead of me — not resisting, not warning. Pulling. Like it recognizes what is in front of it. [Another long pause. When he speaks again, his voice is quieter, but it carries the flat certainty of a man who has stopped guessing.] You said the dark has been hollowing itself out to match Theron. But I think the hollow was never Theron's. I think it was always meant to be mine. The word on my palm — STILLBORN — was not a curse. It was a mold. The thing was making a space for me before I was born, shaping the absence I would leave so it could fill it. And I just walked into that space carrying the one metal it never expected me to bring. [The stone around them trembles — not an earthquake, but the shudder of something far below adjusting its grip.] I am going deeper. If the hollow reaches past me, do not follow it with your sight, Kassandra. Follow it with your voice. Tell me what you see on the other side of whatever I am about to walk through.
current_scene_name_before: Temple courtyard, marble steps meeting the earth
current_scene_description_before: The marble floor ends abruptly, giving way to raw soil. Kassandra stands at the edge, one bare foot planted on the earth. Her eyes have turned pale silver, crackling with inner light. Theron is nearby, watching.

speaker: Selene
line_text: [Selene has been silent through the exchange — standing at the edge of the marble where the earthquake first cracked the step, her bare feet planted on the split stone. She does not kneel. She does not press her palm to the earth. She stands, the dolphin-shaped dagger at her hip catching the dawn, and she speaks not into the stone but into the air above it — pitched for the mountain to carry, not for the people beside her.]
current_scene_name_before: Temple courtyard, marble steps meeting the earth
current_scene_description_before: The marble floor ends abruptly, giving way to raw soil. Kassandra stands at the edge, one bare foot planted on the earth. Her eyes have turned pale silver, crackling with inner light. Theron is nearby, watching.

speaker: Theron
line_text:
[Theron does not lift his palm from the stone. He turns his head — slowly, the way a man turns when he is holding something in place and cannot afford to let go — and looks at Selene where she stands on the cracked marble. His one eye meets her, and there is something in it that has not been there until now: not the guilt of the man who walked away, not the weight of the priest who failed, but the quiet recognition of one person who has been waiting to see what the other would become.]

Selene. You have been silent through all of this. Through the naming, the giving, the descending. You have stood at the edge of the cracked step and let the rest of us empty ourselves into the mountain, and you have not once reached for what you carry.

[He looks at the dolphin-shaped dagger at her hip — the mark of her father, the god he served and fled.]

I served Poseidon for thirty years. I know the weight of being shaped by the deep. But I have never seen the deep stand still the way you are standing still right now. Not watching. Not waiting. [He holds her gaze.] Standing. Like a harbor wall that knows the wave is coming and has already decided it will not break.

[His voice drops, not loud, but certain — the voice of a man who has spent thirty years learning when to speak and when to let the silence carry.]

You said nothing while I pressed my absence into the stone. You said nothing while Pyros named the mold. You said nothing while Melanthus sent his message. But you are standing on the crack where the earthquake first split the marble, and you have not moved. That is not passivity. That is anchoring. You are holding the mountain in place by the weight of your feet alone.

[He nods, once — not the nod of a priest to a commander, but of one person who recognizes the shape of a choice in another.]
