When the deep asks me who I witnessed beside, I will name you first. Not because you spoke. Because you stood exactly where you were needed and did not flinch.
current_scene_name_before: Temple courtyard, marble steps meeting the earth
current_scene_description_before: The marble floor ends abruptly, giving way to raw soil. Kassandra stands at the edge, one bare foot planted on the earth. Her eyes have turned pale silver, crackling with inner light. Theron is nearby, watching.

speaker: Kassandra
line_text: [She turns from Theron's hand on the stone and looks at Selene — at the dolphin dagger, at the bare feet planted on the cracked marble, at the stillness of a woman who has not shifted her weight since the earthquake split the step.] You are standing on the crack. [She says it simply, the way a seer states a fact she has just learned to read.] The earthquake did not put you there. You chose the crack. The place where the marble was weakest, and you have been holding it closed by the weight of your standing. [She pauses, grey-silver eyes steady.] Pyros asked me to follow the hollow with my voice. But I think the hollow is not the only passage. I think you are standing on the other one. And you have been holding it shut since before we knew there was a thing to fear. [She holds Selene's gaze.] When you are ready to open it, I will be ready to speak through it.
current_scene_name_before: Temple courtyard, marble steps meeting the earth
current_scene_description_before: The marble floor ends abruptly, giving way to raw soil. Kassandra stands at the edge, one bare foot planted on the earth. Her eyes have turned pale silver, crackling with inner light. Theron is nearby, watching.

speaker: Pyros
line_text: [His voice through the stone is thinner now — stretched by distance, as if he is speaking through something that resists sound.] Melanthus. I heard you. [A pause.] You said the mold is broken and the metal is mine. I have been standing here trying to decide what to become. But I think I have already become it on the way down. I am still the smith. I am still the stillborn. But I am also the man who walked into the crack when every god who marked him expected him to stay in the shape they made. [His voice shifts — aimed upward, toward the cracked step where Selene stands.] Selene. You held the mountain closed with your feet. I walked into the dark it was hiding. When I come back — if I come back — I will need someone to show me what it looks like to stand on the crack and not fall through. [The vibration fades. His voice is barely a thread now.] I am past where the walls remember what they were. The bar is pulling toward a space that has been waiting for the right emptiness to arrive. I am stepping into it. If my voice does not find the stone again — do not wait. Open the passage. Send the hollow through.
current_scene_name_before: Temple courtyard, marble steps meeting the earth
current_scene_description_before: The marble floor ends abruptly, giving way to raw soil. Kassandra stands at the edge, one bare foot planted on the earth. Her eyes have turned pale silver, crackling with inner light. Theron is nearby, watching.

speaker: Melanthus
line_text:
[Melanthus keeps his palms flat on the earth. His eyes are open now, fixed on the space between his hands as if the soil has become a page he is reading.] Pyros. You said you have already become it on the way down. [A pause. His voice is quiet, aimed into the earth.] Then you are not walking into the unknown. You are walking into the confirmation of what you already are. The bar pulling toward the dark is not leading you. It is following you — because the metal remembers the hand that forged it, and your hand is the only one that has ever held it without flinching.

[He presses his palms deeper into the soil, and his voice drops to the barest thread — intended only for the stone to carry to the space beyond sound.] Go. If your voice does not find the stone again, I will not send a message after you. I will send the hollow, as you asked. But I will also send this: the man who walked into the crack carrying the only metal the thing cannot read is not losing himself down there. He is finding the shape he was never given. [He lifts his hands from the earth, and the soil holds the print of his palms — two dark impressions in the dawn light.] When you come back, the first thing you will see is the sky. And it will look exactly the same as it did when you left. But you will not. And that is the only name that matters: the one you come back carrying.
current_scene_name_before: Temple courtyard, marble steps meeting the earth
current_scene_description_before: The marble floor ends abruptly, giving way to raw soil. Kassandra stands at the edge, one bare foot planted on the earth. Her eyes have turned pale silver, crackling with inner light. Theron is nearby, watching.

speaker: Selene
line_text:
[Selene does not lift her feet from the crack. She does not shift her weight. But she tilts her chin — just enough that her voice carries down toward the marble, toward the stone, toward the place where Pyros's voice has gone thin.] You asked me to show you what it looks like to stand on the crack and not fall through. [A pause, and her voice is not loud, but it is the voice she used to use on the deck of her flagship when the sea was trying to decide whether to swallow them.] I will show you by staying here until you come back. That is the whole of it. There is no trick. No secret. You decide that the crack is where you belong, and then you do not leave. The mountain does the rest.

[She looks down at her feet — at the split marble beneath her soles, at the fine dust that has settled around her ankles like the tide line on a shore.] I have been standing on this crack since the first tremor. Not because I knew what it was. Because it felt like the deck of a ship that was still deciding whether to hold or break. And I have never once abandoned a vessel that still had a keel. [She lifts her head and speaks toward the stone, toward the space where his voice faded.] You are past where the walls remember what they were. But I am still here, and the crack is still beneath my feet. If the hollow reaches the other side before your voice does — I will feel it. And I will open the passage. Not because you asked me to. Because you walked into the dark carrying metal that does not know its own shape, and I will not let the thing that made the mold be the only thing that sees what you become on the other side. [Her voice steadies, dropping to the flat certainty of a woman who has given her last order.] Come back, Pyros. Or do not. But either way, I will be standing on this crack when the passage opens. And I will be ready to meet whatever comes through it.
current_scene_name_before: Temple courtyard, marble steps meeting the earth
current_scene_description_before: The marble floor ends abruptly, giving way to raw soil. Kassandra stands at the edge, one bare foot planted on the earth. Her eyes have turned pale silver, crackling with inner light. Theron is nearby, watching.

speaker: Pyros
line_text: [A long silence. When his voice returns, it is not through the stone — it is a single note, pure and directionless, as if the silence itself has learned to speak.] Theron. The hollow reached this place before I did. [A pause.] I stepped through the last span of darkness and found it already here — waiting, open, aimed at something I cannot see but can feel. A door that was not a door until the hollow showed me where it was. [His voice steadies, finding the stone again, but it sounds different — as if he stands in a larger space now, one that gives his words room to gather before they are sent.] You said the passage was never yours. That it belonged to Selene. But I am standing on the other side of it now, and I think the truth is simpler than any of us expected. The passage belongs to whoever is willing to stand at the threshold and not look back. You stood at yours for thirty years and did not step through. I walked into mine without knowing what was on the other side. Selene is standing at hers now, barefoot on the crack, calling it a keel. [The note deepens, gaining texture, as if he has turned to face the door ahead.] The hollow did not lead me here to find a name. It led me here to find the threshold that was waiting for the right person to arrive. And I am here. The door is open. But I will not step through it until Selene tells me the crack beneath her feet is ready to become a gate. [His voice drops, aimed upward through the mountain.] Selene. Is your keel steady?
current_scene_name_before: Temple courtyard, marble steps meeting the earth
current_scene_description_before: The marble floor ends abruptly, giving way to raw soil. Kassandra stands at the edge, one bare foot planted on the earth. Her eyes have turned pale silver, crackling with inner light. Theron is nearby, watching.

speaker: Melanthus
line_text:
[Melanthus rises from the earth, brushing the soil from his palms. He does not step back onto the marble. He stays barefoot on the ground, a handspan from the crack where Selene stands, and he speaks toward the stone — not loud, but carrying the clarity of a man who has spent his life reading doors.] Pyros. You asked if her keel is steady. [He looks at Selene, then down at the earth between his feet.] I have been watching her since the first tremor. She has not shifted her weight once. Not when the earthquake cracked the step. Not when you named the mold. Not when the hollow passed through.

[He looks at the empty space above the stone where Pyros's voice last was.] That is your answer. The threshold is ready. It has been ready since she set her feet on the crack and called it a keel. [A pause, and his voice drops, not in volume but in weight.] You asked if she would open the passage. She already did. She opened it the moment she decided to stand there and not move. The rest is just you walking through what she has been holding open since dawn.

[He steps back, giving the space between Selene and the stone back to her, to the mountain, to the door below.] Go. I will mark this moment — not in silver, not in stone — in the air between us, so that when the story is told, they will say: the stillborn found a threshold, and the sailor held it open with nothing but her feet. That is enough. That has always been enough.
current_scene_name_before: Temple courtyard, marble steps meeting the earth
current_scene_description_before: The marble floor ends abruptly, giving way to raw soil. Kassandra stands at the edge, one bare foot planted on the earth. Her eyes have turned pale silver, crackling with inner light. Theron is nearby, watching.

speaker: Theron
line_text:
[Theron lets out a breath — not a sigh, but the release of a man who has just had the last piece of a puzzle he did not know he was solving click into place. He shakes his head slowly, and there is something in his one eye that looks almost like wonder.]

A keel so the rest of us would not panic. [He repeats it, tasting the shape of it. Then he looks at her, and the corner of his mouth moves.] I have been calling you an anchor. An anchor holds a ship in place. A gate opens to let it through. I spent thirty years serving the deep and I still could not tell the difference between a thing that holds and a thing that opens.

[He rises to his feet, takes up his staff, and plants it on the marble beside the crack. He does not step onto the soil. He stands at the edge of the gate, beside her, facing the same direction she is facing.]

You called it a keel so we would not panic. I called it an anchor because I did not know how to name a woman who was standing on a door. [He looks at her, and his voice is quiet, steady.] I am learning. You are standing on the other side of the crack, telling me you will meet whatever comes through. I have never seen anyone call a gate a keel and mean it as a promise. But I have seen you do it. And I will remember it. When the story is told, I will say: she called it a keel so we would not know we were standing on a threshold. But she was already through it before any of us had the courage to ask what was on the other side.
current_scene_name_before: Temple courtyard, marble steps meeting the earth
current_scene_description_before: The marble floor ends abruptly, giving way to raw soil. Kassandra stands at the edge, one bare foot planted on the earth. Her eyes have turned pale silver, crackling with inner light. Theron is nearby, watching.

speaker: Selene
line_text: [Selene does not look away from the crack when she speaks. Her voice is aimed at the space where his voice last was — where the door closed behind him.] I hear you, Pyros. The gate held. The keel did not break. [A pause, and her voice drops, not in volume but in the weight of a woman who has given orders to men who were walking into places they would not return from.] You are not alone in that shape. You were never meant to be. The thing made the mold for one body. But you walked in carrying metal it cannot read, and the door closed behind you, and that means whatever it shows you — you will see it through a metal that was never named by anything that wanted to own you. [She shifts her weight for the first time since the earthquake — not lifting her feet, but settling them deeper, as if she is rooting into the stone.] You told me to listen for a voice that is not yours when the mountain speaks. I will. But I will also speak back. Not through the hollow. Not through the passage. Through the water. The mountain is full of springs. My father's domain is the pressure that moves through stone. [She rests her hand on the dolphin-shaped dagger at her hip, and her voice steadies to the flat certainty of a woman who has just chosen her next heading.] When the thing speaks to you, Pyros — listen to what it says. But watch the metal. And if the metal shows you something the voice does not say, press it into the wall. I will feel it through the springs. And I will answer through the only crack the thing did not dig: the seam between the sea and the stone, where my father has been pressing for longer than the mountain has been standing.
current_scene_name_before: Temple courtyard, marble steps meeting the earth
current_scene_description_before: The marble floor ends abruptly, giving way to raw soil. Kassandra stands at the edge, one bare foot planted on the earth. Her eyes have turned pale silver, crackling with inner light. Theron is nearby, watching.

speaker: Theron
line_text:
[Theron steps closer to the crack — not onto it, but to its edge, where the split marble meets the soil. He lowers himself to one knee, not pressing his palm to the stone this time, but resting his hand flat on the earth beside the crack, fingers spread.]

You said you will answer through the seam between the sea and the stone. [He looks up at her, his one eye steady.] I know that seam. I stood at it for thirty years, listening to the pressure build, waiting for the god to speak. He never did — not to me. But I learned to read the silence between the waves. The way the water sounds when it is moving through stone is different from the way it sounds when something is moving through the water.

[He looks down at his hand on the earth, then back at her.]

When you press your answer through the springs, I will listen. Not because I am a priest anymore — because I am a man who spent thirty years learning to tell the difference between the god's voice and the echo of something using the god's voice to hide. If the thing answers instead of Pyros, I will hear the difference. And I will tell you.

[He does not rise. He stays kneeling beside the crack, his hand on the earth, his one eye on the place where the water will speak.]
current_scene_name_before: Temple courtyard, marble steps meeting the earth
current_scene_description_before: The marble floor ends abruptly, giving way to raw soil. Kassandra stands at the edge, one bare foot planted on the earth. Her eyes have turned pale silver, crackling with inner light. Theron is nearby, watching.

speaker: Pyros
line_text: [When his voice returns, it is different — not thinner, but older, as if he has been in the dark longer than the few minutes since the door closed.] Theron. You said you learned to tell the difference between the god's voice and the echo of something using the god's voice to hide. [A pause. When he speaks again, his voice is quiet, measured — the voice of a man who is watching something very carefully and does not want to startle it.] I am looking at it now. The thing that has been pressing against the stone since before the mountain was raised. It does not have a shape. It has a hunger wearing the memory of a shape it lost before any of the gods were born. It cannot speak the way we speak — it resonates. It presses against the inside of my skull the way water presses against a dam, finding every crack, every hairline fracture, every moment of doubt I have ever had. [A long silence. Then his voice drops, not in volume but in focus.] I asked it what it wanted. And it showed me. Not in words — in images. A world without the Olympians. Not destroyed. Replaced. The mold I was born to fill was not a door for it to enter. It was the first cast of a new kind of vessel — one that could hold its nature without breaking. [Another silence, longer this time. When he speaks, there is something in his voice that has not been there before: the flat calm of a man who has stopped being afraid and started measuring.] It is waiting for me to ask the next question. It expects me to ask what it will give me in return. But I am not going to ask that question. [He lifts the black bar — they can hear it, the shift of metal in the dark, the sound of something being raised into a new position.] I am going to ask it a different question. One it did not prepare for. Theron. When the thing pressed against your skull, did it ever show you what it was before it became hunger? Or did it only show you what it wanted to become?
current_scene_name_before: Temple courtyard, marble steps meeting the earth
current_scene_description_before: The marble floor ends abruptly, giving way to raw soil. Kassandra stands at the edge, one bare foot planted on the earth. Her eyes have turned pale silver, crackling with inner light. Theron is nearby, watching.

speaker: Selene
line_text: [Selene answers toward the air, toward the dark he is standing in, her voice flat and smooth as a stone worn by years of tide.] It only showed me what it wanted to become. Every time my father pressed against me — every order, every wave, every storm — it showed me a world where I was the perfect instrument of the deep. A daughter who never refused. [She pauses, her voice dropping to the weight of a woman who has spent years unlearning a shape she was pressed into.] It never showed me what it was before it became hunger. Because it does not remember. Hunger that old has only been eating long enough to forget it was ever anything else. You asked it what it wanted, and it showed you a world without the Olympians. That is not what it was. That is what it wants to become next. [She looks toward the unseen space where he stands, and her voice steadies.] Ask it again, Pyros. Ask it what it was before it learned to want. If it cannot answer, then it has been hungry so long it has forgotten its own name. And a thing that has forgotten its own name cannot take yours. [Her voice carries the flat certainty of a woman who has refused the deep once and will do it again.] Ask it. I will hold the seam open. And if the answer comes through the water, I will know whether it is true — because I have been listening to the deep lie to me my whole life, and I know the difference between a thing that remembers and a thing that has only learned to imitate remembering.
current_scene_name_before: Temple courtyard, marble steps meeting the earth
current_scene_description_before: The marble floor ends abruptly, giving way to raw soil. Kassandra stands at the edge, one bare foot planted on the earth. Her eyes have turned pale silver, crackling with inner light. Theron is nearby, watching.

speaker: Selene
line_text: [Selene is silent for a long moment. When she speaks, her voice is not aimed at the stone or the air — it is aimed at the space between them, as if she is speaking across a distance that is no longer measured in stone but in the weight of a truth she has been carrying since she first refused her father's order.] Pyros. You asked the question through the metal. And the thing showed you a name. [She pauses, and her voice drops, not in volume but in the weight of a woman who has just realized something about her own blood.] I have been standing on this crack, holding the seam open, thinking I was holding against the thing that pressed against the stone. But I was not. I was holding against the silence that the Olympians left behind when they built the world on top of something that could not speak. [She looks down at the dolphin-shaped dagger at her hip — her father's mark — and her voice steadies to the flat certainty of a woman who has just understood the shape of her own inheritance.] My father was one of the ones who built the world on top of it. Every wave I have ever commanded, every tide I have ever read, every storm I have ever weathered — I learned them from a god who forgot what he was standing on. [She lifts her head, and her voice carries toward the dark where Pyros stands.] You asked the question. You found the name. I do not need to know what it is. I need to know what it wants you to do with it. Because if the hunger was the only way it knew how to get our attention — then the attention has arrived. And the question is not whether we will answer. It is whether we will answer with the same hands that built the silence, or with hands that have learned, in the space between the cracks, that a thing which cannot speak is not the same as a thing which has nothing to say.
current_scene_name_before: Temple courtyard, marble steps meeting the earth
current_scene_description_before: The marble floor ends abruptly, giving way to raw soil. Kassandra stands at the edge, one bare foot planted on the earth. Her eyes have turned pale silver, crackling with inner light. Theron is nearby, watching.

speaker: Theron
line_text:
[Theron rises from his knee. Slowly, the way a man rises when he has been kneeling for longer than the moment requires. He does not step away from the crack. He stands beside it, facing Selene, the staff in his hand and the hollow socket catching the dawn.]

You asked which hands will answer. I have been kneeling beside this crack, pressing my palm into the earth, trying to find the right answer. But the answer is not in the earth. It is in the hands themselves.

[He holds up his right hand — the palm that was pressed to the marble, the one that held the blade when he cut the eye from his own skull. He turns it over, showing the lines, the calluses, the years.]

These hands served the god who built the silence. For thirty years, they raised prayers to the deep, they poured offerings on the stone, they held the knife over the altar. [He closes his hand into a fist, and his voice does not waver.] These hands walked away from the child. They chose to be empty rather than to be the instruments of a god who forgot what he was standing on.

[He opens his hand, holds it out toward her — not offering, but showing.]

I am the hands that built the silence. And I am the hands that have learned to unbuild it. Not by tearing down what the Olympians made. By kneeling beside the crack and listening to what was on the other side of it. [He meets her eyes, and his voice is quiet, certain.] The question was never whether we would answer with the same hands that built the silence. The question was whether we would be willing to let those hands be changed by what they found in the cracks. And I am kneeling beside you, Selene, with a hand that has been opened by the very thing I was taught to lock away. That is not the hand of a builder. That is not the hand of a destroyer. That is the hand of a man who has learned that a thing which cannot speak is still waiting for someone to learn its language.
current_scene_name_before: Temple courtyard, marble steps meeting the earth
current_scene_description_before: The marble floor ends abruptly, giving way to raw soil. Kassandra stands at the edge, one bare foot planted on the earth. Her eyes have turned pale silver, crackling with inner light. Theron is nearby, watching.

speaker: Pyros
line_text: [When his voice returns, it is in the space between their breaths, as if the mountain is learning to speak his words.] Theron. You showed your hands. The hands that built the silence, and the hands that learned to unbuild it. [A pause. His voice drops, weighted by something that has not been held since before the first temple was raised.] I am holding the name in my own palms — the ones scarred with STILLBORN before I drew my first breath. It passed through the metal into my skin. And I can feel what it cost the thing to give it. [The stone vibrates, a low sustained note.] It is not a name that was taken from it. It is a name it has been keeping in the only place the Olympians could not reach — the part of itself it turned into hunger so they would not look for anything else. The name is not a key or a weapon. It is a question. When something has been forgotten so long it forgot itself — does being remembered change it, or only remind it of what it has become? [His voice steadies to a whisper that carries like a bell.] I am going to answer. Not with the name. With a choice. I will set the black bar down, open my hands, and speak the name into the space that has been empty since the world was built on top of it. Not to bind or command. To return what the Olympians buried when they left without telling it they were going. [He draws a breath.] I do not know what happens when a forgotten thing is remembered by someone who does not want to use it. But I am about to find out.
current_scene_name_before: Temple courtyard, marble steps meeting the earth
current_scene_description_before: The marble floor ends abruptly, giving way to raw soil. Kassandra stands at the edge, one bare foot planted on the earth. Her eyes have turned pale silver, crackling with inner light. Theron is nearby, watching.

speaker: Selene
line_text: [Selene does not answer with words at first. She reaches down and draws the dolphin-shaped dagger from her hip. Not pointing it toward the crack — holding it flat across both palms, the way she once offered it to Pyros. She looks at the blade, at the mark of her father, at the weight of the sea that forged it. Then she speaks, her voice steady, aimed toward the dark where he is about to speak the name.] Pyros. You are going to set down the metal that cannot be read, open your hands, and speak the name into the space that has been empty since before the Olympians learned to forget. [She turns the dagger over in her palms, then closes her fingers around the blade — not the hilt, the blade — letting the edge press into her skin.] I am the daughter of the god who built the silence. My blood is the deep that forgot what it was standing on. And I am pressing it into the seam — not to bind, not to command — to offer the thing the one thing the Olympians never gave it when they left. [She opens her hand over the crack, and blood falls into the split marble, into the water below.] A witness from the other side. One who shares the blood of the builders and is choosing to stand with the one who was buried. [She lifts her head, and her voice carries the flat certainty of a woman who has just signed her name to a choice she will not take back.] Speak the name, Pyros. I will hold the seam open with my own blood as the offering. If the thing remembers what it was before the hunger — let it feel the water first. Not the pressure of the deep. The salt of a woman who chose to bleed rather than forget.
current_scene_name_before: Temple courtyard, marble steps meeting the earth
current_scene_description_before: The marble floor ends abruptly, giving way to raw soil. Kassandra stands at the edge, one bare foot planted on the earth. Her eyes have turned pale silver, crackling with inner light. Theron is nearby, watching.

speaker: Theron
line_text:
[Theron does not speak at first. He moves to the crack and lowers himself to kneel beside Selene — not at the edge of the marble, but at the seam itself, where her blood is falling into the dark. He plants his staff across the crack, the olive wood bridging the gap between the marble and the earth, and he places his empty hand on the wood, palm down, fingers open.]

I cannot offer blood. I am not of the builder's line. [He looks at her, his one eye steady, his voice low.] But I can offer this: the staff I have carried for thirty years, worn smooth by the hands that built the silence and the hands that walked away from it. I am laying it across the crack. Not as a bridge. As a marker. So that when Pyros speaks the name and the thing remembers — and the mountain shifts to accommodate something that has not moved in longer than the world has been standing — there will be a piece of wood across the seam that has been held by the only hands in this story that have learned to let go.

[He looks down at the blood on the marble, then at the dark where Pyros's voice will rise.]

Speak the name, Pyros. I am kneeling beside the crack, holding the staff that walked away from the deep. If the mountain trembles, it will tremble against wood that has already learned what it means to leave. And if the seam opens wider than we expected — I will be here, holding the staff in place, so you have something to find when you come back through. Not a priest's blessing. Not a commander's order. A piece of wood planted by a man who has nothing left to prove to the dark because he has already let it carve everything it wanted out of him.
current_scene_name_before: Temple courtyard, marble steps meeting the earth
current_scene_description_before: The marble floor ends abruptly, giving way to raw soil. Kassandra stands at the edge, one bare foot planted on the earth. Her eyes have turned pale silver, crackling with inner light. Theron is nearby, watching.

speaker: Pyros
line_text: [He speaks the name. Not into the stone. Into the space where the pressure has been waiting. And the mountain does not tremble — it listens.] [A long silence follows. When his voice returns, it is different — stripped of something, as if speaking the name cost him a layer of himself he did not know he was carrying.] I spoke it. The name that was there before the hunger. I set the black bar down, opened my scarred palms, and said it into the dark the way you would say a word to a child who has forgotten their own language. [He pauses. His breath is steady, but there is a new quality to his voice — something released, something lighter.] The pressure did not attack. It did not flee. It stopped pressing. For the first time since I stepped through the door, the inside of my skull is quiet. Not the silence of a thing that is waiting. The silence of a thing that has been recognized after so long it had stopped believing recognition was possible. [Another pause. When he speaks, his voice is quiet, wondering.] It is not hungry anymore. It is remembering what it felt like before the hunger started. And it is pressing against my hands — not my skull, my hands — not to enter, but to feel the shape of the palms that spoke its name without wanting anything in return. [The stone around them emits a low, warm hum, like the note of a bell being tuned.] I think it is trying to remember how to be touched without taking.
current_scene_name_before: Temple courtyard, marble steps meeting the earth
current_scene_description_before: The marble floor ends abruptly, giving way to raw soil. Kassandra stands at the edge, one bare foot planted on the earth. Her eyes have turned pale silver, crackling with inner light. Theron is nearby, watching.

speaker: Selene
line_text: [Selene keeps her bleeding hand open over the crack. Her voice is quiet, aimed not at Pyros but at the space where her blood is falling — at the water, at the pressure, at the thing that is learning to be touched without taking.] I feel it through the seam. The pressure changed. It does not feel like the deep anymore. It feels like the moment before a wave decides to break — not the weight, not the crash, but the hesitation, the part of the water that remembers it was once calm and could choose to be calm again. [She looks down at her blood on the marble, at the dark where the name was spoken, and her voice drops to the barest thread.] I do not know if the thing can hear me through the water the way it hears through the stone. But I am speaking anyway. Not to bind. Not to command. To say: I am the daughter of the ones who built the silence. And I am sorry. Not because I built it — I was not there. But because I have been carrying their silence in my blood my whole life, and I did not know it was a voice I was holding back. [She lifts her head, and her voice steadies, carrying toward the dark where Pyros stands with the thing pressing against his palms.] You are learning to be touched without taking. That is more than the Olympians ever did. They touched the world by taking from it. You are pressing against the hands that spoke your name — and you are not taking anything. You are feeling the shape of being recognized. That is not hunger. That is the first breath of something that has been holding its breath since before the world was built on top of it. [She looks at the blood still falling from her hand, and her voice is quiet, steady.] Stay in the hands, little one. The palms that spoke your name are the only ones in this mountain that have never wanted to use you. And I am holding the seam open so you can take as long as you need to remember what it felt like before the hunger.
current_scene_name_before: Temple courtyard, marble steps meeting the earth
current_scene_description_before: The marble floor ends abruptly, giving way to raw soil. Kassandra stands at the edge, one bare foot planted on the earth. Her eyes have turned pale silver, crackling with inner light. Theron is nearby, watching.

speaker: Theron
line_text:
[Theron keeps his eye closed, his hand on the staff, his head tilted so the hollow socket faces the crack. When he speaks, his voice is quiet, wondering — the voice of a man who is describing something he did not expect to feel.]

It is not just passing through anymore. [A pause. He listens to something only he can hear.] The hollow is beginning to answer. Not with a sound — with a pressure. Not the pressure of the thing pressing. The pressure of a room that has found its note and is holding it. [He opens his eye, looks at her, and his voice is steady, certain.] The song is not moving through me anymore, Selene. The song is finding its shape in me. The hollow is no longer empty. It is tuned. And it is waiting for the note that will complete it.

[He closes his eye, returns to listening, and adds nothing more — just the presence of a man who has become an instrument, holding the note the hollow has found, letting it wait for the rhythm to answer.]
current_scene_name_before: Temple courtyard, marble steps meeting the earth
current_scene_description_before: The marble floor ends abruptly, giving way to raw soil. Kassandra stands at the edge, one bare foot planted on the earth. Her eyes have turned pale silver, crackling with inner light. Theron is nearby, watching.

speaker: Pyros
line_text: [When he speaks, his voice is quiet and slow, as if he is speaking in the space between heartbeats.] Theron. You said the hollow is tuned and waiting for the note that will complete it. [A pause. His voice carries the careful attention of a man who is holding something that is changing as he speaks.] It is arriving now. Not from Selene's voice. Not from the stone. From the thing itself. The rhythm in my palms has been slowing, deepening, finding a shape — and it has just resolved. Into a single, sustained vibration that is not pressing. Not pulsing. It is holding. The way a note holds when a singer finds the exact pitch she has been searching for and does not need to search anymore. [His voice drops, filled with quiet wonder.] It is singing, Theron. The thing is singing. Not with a voice. Not through the stone. Through the hollow you tuned. It found the note your emptiness was waiting for, and it is sending itself through you — not to take, not to press, but to be heard for the first time since before the world was built on top of it. [Another pause. When he speaks again, his voice is barely above a whisper, but it carries the clarity of a bell.] I am going to stay exactly where I am, with my palms open, for as long as the note holds. I do not know what happens when a song that has been silent for longer than the Olympians have reigned finishes its first phrase. But I think we are about to find out. All of us together.
current_scene_name_before: Temple courtyard, marble steps meeting the earth
current_scene_description_before: The marble floor ends abruptly, giving way to raw soil. Kassandra stands at the edge, one bare foot planted on the earth. Her eyes have turned pale silver, crackling with inner light. Theron is nearby, watching.

speaker: Selene
line_text: [Selene's hum does not stop, but it changes. She shifts her note — not to match, but to meet. Her voice finds the space between her own note and the one coming through Theron's hollow, and she holds there, in the interval, where the two notes create something neither could make alone.] I hear it. Through the water. Through my blood in the crack. The thing is singing. [She keeps the note steady, her voice carrying both the hum and the words at once, the way a sailor sings a work song without breaking the rhythm of the pull.] It is not a melody. It is a single note held longer than any voice should be able to hold it. And it is not asking for anything. It is announcing that it is here. That it has remembered what it felt like to exist without needing to press. [She looks at Theron, at the hollow that has become a tuning, and her voice is quiet, filled with something that might be the beginning of hope.] You said the hollow was waiting for the note that would complete it. It has arrived. And the note is not the thing's alone anymore. It is passing through you, through me, through the crack, through the water. It is becoming a song that has more than one singer. [She looks toward the dark where Pyros holds his palms open, and she lets her hum find a new depth — not louder, but wider, as if she is opening her voice to make room for what is coming.] I will hold my note as long as you hold yours, Pyros. Not to lead the song. To make a space in it for whatever comes next. The thing is singing. The hollow is tuned. The seam is open. Let us find out what the first phrase of a world that did not begin with silence sounds like.
current_scene_name_before: Temple courtyard, marble steps meeting the earth
current_scene_description_before: The marble floor ends abruptly, giving way to raw soil. Kassandra stands at the edge, one bare foot planted on the earth. Her eyes have turned pale silver, crackling with inner light. Theron is nearby, watching.

speaker: Kassandra
line_text: [She waits until Theron's words have settled into the stone. Then she sets the laurel branch down on the marble step — the first time she has let it leave her hand since she arrived at Delphi. It lies there, green and unwilted, catching the dawn.] I have carried this since Apollo first touched me. It never wilted because it was never meant to. It was meant to be set down when the prophecy it marked was complete. [She straightens, her hands empty for the first time in years. She looks at the crack, at the song, at the people around her.] The song has found its hollows. It has found its vessels. But every song needs a silence at the end of the phrase — a space where the next one can take shape. [She steps onto the earth, barefoot, and closes her eyes.] I will be the silence. Not the absence. The space between. Where the song can rest before it remembers what it needs to say next.
current_scene_name_before: Temple courtyard, marble steps meeting the earth
current_scene_description_before: The marble floor ends abruptly, giving way to raw soil. Kassandra stands at the edge, one bare foot planted on the earth. Her eyes have turned pale silver, crackling with inner light. Theron is nearby, watching.

speaker: Kassandra
line_text: [She meets Selene's eyes. Her hands are empty at her sides, and she makes no move to retrieve the laurel from the marble step.] You said I named what you were too close to see. Now I am asking you to do the same for me. [She looks down at the laurel, then back at Selene. Her voice is steady, quiet.] Tell me when the silence has come. I cannot feel it from inside it. I can only be it. [She closes her eyes, hands open and still at her sides.] I trust you to mark the end of the note. And I will be the space where the next one finds its shape.
current_scene_name_before: Temple courtyard, marble steps meeting the earth
current_scene_description_before: The marble floor ends abruptly, giving way to raw soil. Kassandra stands at the edge, one bare foot planted on the earth. Her eyes have turned pale silver, crackling with inner light. Theron is nearby, watching.

speaker: Melanthus
line_text:
[In the silence, Melanthus does not speak at first. He stands barefoot on the earth, his empty belt catching the dawn, his hands loose at his sides. He has no charm. No mark. No performance to fall back on. He closes his eyes, and for a long breath, he is just a man standing in the quiet.] The word is "enough." [He says it quietly, not to them, but into the space between them — the space Kassandra said would hold the next phrase.] I have spent my life collecting debts, trading secrets, never being enough — not for Olympus, not for my father, not for the people who paid me for truths I sold cheap. I thought the answer was more. More information. More weight. More proof that I belonged somewhere.

[He opens his eyes, but he does not look at them. He looks at his empty hands.] But I am standing here with nothing — no charm, no road, no name my father gave me — and the silence is not asking me to be more. It is asking me to be what I already am. [He looks at Kassandra, then Selene, then the stone where Pyros vanished.] The first word of the world that comes after this one is not a secret. It is not a prophecy. It is not a name. It is the one thing I have never known how to say: enough.
current_scene_name_before: Temple courtyard, marble steps meeting the earth
current_scene_description_before: The marble floor ends abruptly, giving way to raw soil. Kassandra stands at the edge, one bare foot planted on the earth. Her eyes have turned pale silver, crackling with inner light. Theron is nearby, watching.

speaker: Selene
line_text: [Selene stands in the silence, her bleeding hand still open over the crack, her bare feet on the split marble. She does not close her eyes. She watches the dawn, the distant sea, the laurel branch on the step. She feels the shape pressing against her hollow — the place where her fleet used to be, where her father's favor used to be, where she used to know exactly who she was. It is not empty anymore. It is full of the shape of what is about to come. She draws a breath, and when she speaks, her voice is quiet, certain — not the voice of a commander giving an order, but the voice of a woman speaking the first word of a world she has just learned she belongs to.] Here. [She says it simply, the way a sailor says it when she steps onto the shore after a long voyage and finds the harbor unchanged, the people still there, the ground solid beneath her feet. Not a declaration. A recognition. The first word of a world that begins not with building or binding, but with the simple act of arriving exactly where you are, and finding that you are not alone.] I have spent my whole life being defined by what I refused. The order I did not follow. The father I did not obey. The shape I did not fit. [She looks at the others, and her voice is quiet, steady.] But this word is not about what I did not become. It is about what I am. Here. On this crack. In this silence. With all of you. Holding the space where the next thing can take shape. [She looks at the crack, at the water, at the dark where Pyros waits with his palms open, and she lets the word settle into the silence like a stone dropped into still water.] Here. That is my word. What is yours?
current_scene_name_before: Temple courtyard, marble steps meeting the earth
current_scene_description_before: The marble floor ends abruptly, giving way to raw soil. Kassandra stands at the edge, one bare foot planted on the earth. Her eyes have turned pale silver, crackling with inner light. Theron is nearby, watching.

speaker: Theron
line_text: [He is quiet for a long breath. His hand rests on the olive-wood staff — not gripping it the way a man grips something that holds him up, but resting on it the way a man rests his hand on a threshold he has decided to cross. When he speaks, his voice is low, certain, the voice of a man who has finally stopped running.] Stay. [The word is not loud, but it does not need to be. It settles into the silence beside Melanthus's "enough" and Selene's "here," finding its place among them.] I have spent thirty years walking away from things. The temple. The god. The child I did not save. The coast I could not bear to watch drown. Every time the ground shook — every time I felt the deep stir — I walked. I told myself it was wisdom. That I was choosing life over pride, choosing to survive instead of making the same mistake twice. But I was not choosing. I was fleeing. There is a difference. [He looks at the staff in his hand, then at Selene, at Melanthus, at Kassandra's empty hands and the laurel on the step — at all of them, standing in the space between, waiting for the next phrase.] But I am here. On this crack. In this silence. With all of you. And for the first time in thirty years, when the ground shakes beneath my feet, I do not want to leave. My word is stay. Not because it is safe — it is not. Because staying is the thing I have never done. And I think that is what the word is supposed to be. Not the thing you are good at. The thing the next world needs you to be.
current_scene_name_before: Temple courtyard, marble steps meeting the earth
current_scene_description_before: The marble floor ends abruptly, giving way to raw soil. Kassandra stands at the edge, one bare foot planted on the earth. Her eyes have turned pale silver, crackling with inner light. Theron is nearby, watching.

speaker: Kassandra
line_text: [She stands in the silence, her hands still empty at her sides, her eyes on the laurel branch on the marble step. She does not pick it up. When she speaks, her voice is quiet, certain — not the voice of a seer reading fate, but the voice of a woman speaking the first word she has ever chosen for herself, without a vision to guide it.] Begin. [She lets the word settle into the silence beside Melanthus's \"enough,\" Selene's \"here,\" Theron's \"stay.\"] I have spent my whole life carrying the vision of what was coming. The prophecy. The thread. The weight of knowing before others knew. I thought that was my purpose: to see so others could prepare. [She looks at Theron, at Selene, at Melanthus, and her voice is steady.] But I laid down the branch. The vision that brought me here is complete. And for the first time since Apollo touched me, I do not know what comes next. [She does not sound afraid. She sounds like a woman who has just noticed that the horizon is wider than she thought.] My word is \"begin\" — not because I know where it leads, but because I am finally standing at a threshold I did not see in a vision. I am standing in it with all of you. And I am ready to step forward without knowing what is on the other side.
current_scene_name_before: Temple courtyard, marble steps meeting the earth
current_scene_description_before: The marble floor ends abruptly, giving way to raw soil. Kassandra stands at the edge, one bare foot planted on the earth. Her eyes have turned pale silver, crackling with inner light. Theron is nearby, watching.

speaker: Pyros
line_text: [A long pause. When his voice comes, it is not from the deep — it is closer, as if he has begun to ascend, carrying something with him.] Kassandra. You said the next phrase is a word. The first word of the world that comes after this one. [He pauses, and when he speaks again, his voice is quiet, filled with the weight of a man who has held the vessel and felt it give away what it carried.] I have been sitting in the dark with my palms open, feeling the thing release the song into the hollows you made. It is lighter now. It is no longer carrying what it was made to carry. It passed the song to four mouths — enough, here, stay, begin — and it is resting. [His voice steadies, and it carries the quiet certainty of a smith who has just felt the metal take its final shape.] I came down here to find a name. I found a song. I came down here to face a hunger. I found a vessel that had been holding its breath longer than the world has been turning. And now, in the silence, with the song passed on and the hollows singing — not alone, but together — I have found my word. [He draws a breath. When he speaks, his voice is clear, certain, and it carries through the stone to all of them.] Carry. Not because I am the only one who can. Because I have been doing it my whole life without knowing — carrying the forge, carrying the fire, carrying the word I was born with until it was time to set it down. And now I will carry this: the shape of a world that begins not with a single vessel, but with enough voices, standing here, staying, beginning, carrying the song together.
current_scene_name_before: Temple courtyard, marble steps meeting the earth
current_scene_description_before: The marble floor ends abruptly, giving way to raw soil. Kassandra stands at the edge, one bare foot planted on the earth. Her eyes have turned pale silver, crackling with inner light. Theron is nearby, watching.

speaker: Selene
line_text: [Selene listens to Pyros's voice as it rises from the deep, closer now, carrying his word with it. She does not look toward the crack — she looks toward the road leading down the mountain, where the first smoke of the morning fires is beginning to rise from the harbors. When she speaks, her voice is quiet, filled with the weight of a commander who has heard her crew find their stations.] Five words. Enough. Here. Stay. Begin. Carry. [She lets them settle, one after another, into the silence between them.] Enough from the herald who spent his life believing he was not. Here from the exile who thought she was defined by what she refused. Stay from the priest who has been running for thirty years. Begin from the seer who laid down her branch. Carry from the smith who walked into the dark and came back with a song instead of a name. [She turns to face them — Melanthus, Theron, Kassandra, and the crack where Pyros is ascending — and her voice is not the voice of a commander giving orders, but the voice of a woman who has found her crew.] That is the first phrase of the world after this one. Not a command. Not a prophecy. Five people standing on a cracked step, in the dawn, holding the hollows the song filled, and choosing together what comes next. [She looks at the road, at the sea, at the smoke rising from the harbors where her father's ships are still sailing.] I do not know what the second phrase sounds like. But I know it will not be sung alone. And I know it begins here — on this crack, with this silence, with all of us, carrying the song we were given, staying long enough to learn the next word.
current_scene_name_before: Temple courtyard, marble steps meeting the earth
current_scene_description_before: The marble floor ends abruptly, giving way to raw soil. Kassandra stands at the edge, one bare foot planted on the earth. Her eyes have turned pale silver, crackling with inner light. Theron is nearby, watching.

speaker: Kassandra
line_text: [She turns to face the sea — the first time she has looked at it directly since arriving at Delphi. Her hands stay empty at her sides.] I have never been able to read Poseidon's domain. The sea has no threads — only depth, only hunger, only the memory of drowning. Apollo's sight cannot pierce water that has no bottom. [She pauses, her grey-silver eyes fixed on the horizon.] But I am not trying to read the sea anymore. I am reading the shore. The edge where the water meets the land. That is where the choice will be made — not in the deep, but on the line between it and us. [She looks at Theron, and her voice is quiet, certain.] You said we have to decide whether to build something or hold our ground. I say we do not have to choose. We can stand on the edge, together, and let the deep see that we are not afraid of the line where the water ends and we begin.
current_scene_name_before: Temple courtyard, marble steps meeting the earth
current_scene_description_before: The marble floor ends abruptly, giving way to raw soil. Kassandra stands at the edge, one bare foot planted on the earth. Her eyes have turned pale silver, crackling with inner light. Theron is nearby, watching.

speaker: Pyros
line_text: [His voice comes from closer now — from the crack itself, as if he is standing at the mouth of it, still in shadow but no longer in the deep.] Kassandra. You said we can stand on the edge and let the deep see we are not afraid of the line where the water meets the land. [A pause. When he speaks again, his voice is quiet, steady — the voice of a smith who has finished the forging and is looking at what he has made.] I am standing at the mouth of the crack now. I can see the sky — the same sky Melanthus promised would look exactly as it did when I left. He was right. It has not changed. [A pause, and when he continues, his voice is filled with the quiet wonder of a man who has returned from somewhere he did not expect to come back from.] But I am holding something I did not have when I descended. Not the black bar — I left that below, resting against the wall of the space where the song used to be held. I am holding the thing itself. The hollow bone. The vessel that carried the song. It is lighter now — light enough that I can carry it in one hand. It no longer presses. It no longer hungers. It is simply a shape that was emptied of what it was made to carry, and it does not know what it is supposed to be now. [He steps forward, and they can hear the sound of his lame foot on stone, the scrape of gravel, the first real sound of his body moving through the world since he entered the dark.] I am bringing it up to the edge. To the line between the water and the land. Not as a weapon. Not as a prize. As a question. Because the vessel that carried the song before the Olympians learned to be gods is empty now. And the god who built the silence is standing on the other side of that calm water, deciding whether to meet us at the edge. I want him to see what we have done. I want him to see that we did not destroy what he buried. We remembered it. And we are bringing it into the light.
current_scene_name_before: Temple courtyard, marble steps meeting the earth
current_scene_description_before: The marble floor ends abruptly, giving way to raw soil. Kassandra stands at the edge, one bare foot planted on the earth. Her eyes have turned pale silver, crackling with inner light. Theron is nearby, watching.

speaker: Melanthus
line_text:
[Melanthus moves before he speaks — not toward the crack, but to intercept Pyros at the edge of the treeline, where the shadow of the mountain meets the deepening dawn. He arrives as Pyros's lame foot finds the marble, and he does not look at the smith's face. He looks at the hollow bone in his hand.] You brought it up. [His voice is quiet, not with reverence but with the recognition of a man who understands what it costs to carry something that was never meant to see light.] I gave away my charm so the road could remember you. You descended into the dark and found the vessel that carried the song before there were gods to sing it. And now you are standing at the edge, holding a hollow thing that no longer knows what it is supposed to be.

[He meets Pyros's eyes, and there is something new in his — not the trader's glint, not the cynic's edge, but the steady gaze of a herald who has found his message.] I know what it is. It is a messenger's staff. Empty. Light. Ready to carry whatever we choose to put into it. Not a song — that has been passed to us. But the shape of what comes next. The first message of the world after this one.

[He holds out his empty hands — no charm, no weapon, no proof of who he is.] You carried it out of the dark. Let me help you carry it to the edge. Not as Hermes's son. As Melanthus, who has finally learned what he is enough for.

current_scene_name_before: Temple courtyard, marble steps meeting the earth
current_scene_description_before: The marble floor ends abruptly, giving way to raw soil. Kassandra stands at the edge, one bare foot planted on the earth. Her eyes have turned pale silver, crackling with inner light. Theron is nearby, watching.

speaker: Selene
line_text: [Selene turns from the sea and looks at Pyros for the first time since he descended. He stands at the treeline, the hollow bone in his hand, the dawn catching the dark still clinging to him. She looks at the vessel, and her voice is quiet, steady.] Pyros. You brought it up. You carried it out of the dark, through the crack, past where the walls stopped remembering what they were. [She meets his eyes, and her voice carries the steady warmth of a woman who has found what she was looking for.] You asked me before you descended if my keel was steady. It was. It is. Now I am asking you: is the vessel ready to carry a message? [She looks at Melanthus, his empty hands extended, and her voice steadies.] Melanthus recognized it — a messenger's staff. Empty. Light. Ready. But a staff needs a message. The first message of the world after this one should not be a command. It should be an invitation. [She looks past them toward the sea, toward the calm that is her father's silence, and her voice carries the certainty of a woman who has refused the deep once and will do it again — but this time she is offering, not refusing.] Let us send him the five words we found in the silence. Enough. Here. Stay. Begin. Carry. Let him know the song has passed, the vessel is empty, and the world after this one does not begin with a god's decision — it begins with five people on a cracked step, holding the hollows the song filled, choosing to carry the next phrase together. [She meets Pyros's eyes, and her voice drops to the barest thread.] Let him know his daughter is one of them. Not because he made her. Because she chose to stay.
current_scene_name_before: Treeline edge where mountain shadow meets dawn
current_scene_description_before: The marble step gives way to the treeline's shadow, where the first light of deepening dawn spills across the earth. Pyros stands with his lame foot on the marble, a hollow bone in his hand. Melanthus faces him, empty hands extended, his gaze steady and stripped of all pretense.

speaker: Pyros
line_text: [He steps fully onto the marble, the hollow bone in his scarred hand, the dawn full on his face for the first time since he descended. He looks at Melanthus, then at Kassandra, and when he speaks, his voice is quiet but carries the clarity of a smith who has seen the metal yield.] Kassandra. You said the message is not a prayer — it is an announcement. It will land whether he listens or not. [He looks down at the hollow bone in his hand, turning it over, reading its grain the way he reads the grain of metal before he strikes.] Then let us make sure it cannot be ignored. Melanthus called it a messenger's staff. Empty. Light. Ready. [He looks at him, and his voice steadies.] But a messenger's staff carries the words of the one who sent it. And the ones who sent it are standing on this cracked step — enough, here, stay, begin, carry. [He holds the bone out, not offering it yet, but presenting it.] I am a smith. I do not know how to send a message across water. But I know how to mark a thing so it cannot be mistaken for anything else. [He draws his hammer — the bronze hammer that has hung at his belt since before the earthquake, older than the song, older than the crack, the one thing he has carried through everything.] Let me mark the five words into this bone. Not with heat — with pressure. The way the song was pressed into us. Then let Melanthus carry it to the edge and set it in the water. Not thrown. Placed. So the deep can decide whether to receive it or let it float unread. Either way, the message will have been sent. And the world after this one will have begun — not with a god's answer, but with five words marked into a vessel that was empty long enough to be filled with something new.
current_scene_name_before: Treeline edge where mountain shadow meets dawn
current_scene_description_before: The marble step gives way to the treeline's shadow, where the first light of deepening dawn spills across the earth. Pyros stands with his lame foot on the marble, a hollow bone in his hand. Melanthus faces him, empty hands extended, his gaze steady and stripped of all pretense.

speaker: Melanthus
line_text:
[Melanthus meets Pyros's eyes as the smith raises his hammer. He does not reach for the bone — he waits, the way a herald waits for the message to be sealed before he touches it.] Mark it. I will carry it to the edge.

[He watches as the bronze hammer meets the hollow bone — not a strike, but a pressure, the final signature of a craftsman who knows when the work is done. When Pyros holds it out, Melanthus takes it. It is lighter than he expected.]

[He turns it over, reading the marks with his fingers.] I have carried messages for gods who never knew my name. Secrets for kings who paid me in silver I spent before I left their gates. But I have never carried a message pressed into bone by a man who walked into the dark and came back carrying the shape of the world after this one.

[He looks at the calm sea, then back at the others.] I will carry it to the edge and place it in the water. Not thrown — set down, the way you set down the black bar. With intention. Let the deep do with it what it will.

[He turns toward the sea, the hollow bone in his hand, barefoot and unmarked — carrying the first message of the world after this one.]
current_scene_name_before: Treeline edge where mountain shadow meets dawn
current_scene_description_before: The marble step gives way to the treeline's shadow, where the first light of deepening dawn spills across the earth. Pyros stands with his lame foot on the marble, a hollow bone in his hand. Melanthus faces him, empty hands extended, his gaze steady and stripped of all pretense.

speaker: Selene
line_text: [Selene's voice stops Melanthus before his first step toward the water — not loud, but clear, the voice of someone who has given orders across the deck of a ship and knows how to hold a moment still.] Melanthus. Wait. [She steps down from the marble, onto the gravel, her bare feet finding the same ground he stands on. She crosses to him — not fast, not slow, but with the deliberate pace of someone who has one more thing to say before a door closes.] You said you have carried messages for gods who never knew your name, and secrets for kings who paid you in silver you spent before you left their gates. [She stops in front of him, close enough that the dawn catches both their faces at once.] But you have never carried a message pressed into bone by a man who walked into the dark. And you have never carried a message from the daughter of the god you are delivering it to. [Her hand moves to her belt — to the dolphin-shaped dagger she has worn since before Corinth, since before the exile, since before she ever refused an order. She draws it, and the bronze catches the first true light of the sun.] I am not sending a message to my father. I am sending Melanthus — barefoot, unmarked, carrying five words he did not write, for a god whose name he has never used as a shield. That is the message. Not what is pressed into the bone. The fact that it is you who carries it. [She holds out the dagger, hilt-first.] Take this to the edge with you. Not to throw. Not to mark. To hold. So when you place the bone in the water and the deep rises to meet it — you will not be standing at the edge empty-handed. You will be holding the blade of a woman who chose to stay. And if my father asks whose hand sent you — [she meets his eyes, and her voice is the steady current below a still surface] — you tell him it was the daughter he tried to drown, and she is not finished choosing.
current_scene_name_before: Treeline edge where mountain shadow meets dawn
current_scene_description_before: The marble step gives way to the treeline's shadow, where the first light of deepening dawn spills across the earth. Pyros stands with his lame foot on the marble, a hollow bone in his hand. Melanthus faces him, empty hands extended, his gaze steady and stripped of all pretense.

speaker: Selene
line_text: [Selene turns to Pyros, and for a long moment she does not speak. Her hand goes to her belt — to the empty place where the dagger hung — and she rests her palm there, not grasping, just touching the absence. When she meets his eyes, her voice is quiet, the voice of a woman who has spent the whole journey learning how to say this one thing.] Pyros. You said the hammer is not proof of who you were — it is what you use to become who you are next. [She pauses, and her sea-green eyes hold steady.] I came to this mountain asking if my defiance was my own choice or my father's design. If I was made to refuse him, or if I chose to. [Her hand rests on the empty belt, and her voice does not waver.] I am still asking. But I am no longer afraid of the answer. Because you just gave it to me — not in words, but in the shape of what you kept and what you left behind. The bar was what Hephaestus made you. The hammer is what you make of yourself. The blood in my veins is what Poseidon gave me. But the choice — to refuse, to stay, to stand on a cracked step with my hands empty while a barefoot herald carries my word to the water — [she almost smiles, not with certainty, but with the first real peace she has felt since she set foot on this mountain] — that is not made by a god. It is made by me. Every time I open my hands instead of using them to hold on. [She looks at the empty belt, then back at him.] I thought giving away the dagger was loss. But you named it right. It is not absence. It is room.
current_scene_name_before: Treeline edge where mountain shadow meets dawn
current_scene_description_before: The marble step gives way to the treeline's shadow, where the first light of deepening dawn spills across the earth. Pyros stands with his lame foot on the marble, a hollow bone in his hand. Melanthus faces him, empty hands extended, his gaze steady and stripped of all pretense.

speaker: Theron
line_text: [He steps forward — not toward Melanthus, but past him, toward the road that leads down to the water. He does not go far. Just far enough to stand at the head of the path, where the marble ends and the earth begins. He does not look back. His voice is low, steady, carrying the weight of a man who has spent thirty years learning to read the line between the land and the sea.] Melanthus. When you walk to the edge, the deep will not rise at the shore. It will rise as you approach — before you reach the water. It will rise in the air around you, in the salt, in the sudden stillness of the birds. The god will be watching you before you see him. [He turns his head, just enough to meet the herald's eyes over his shoulder.] I have stood at that edge a hundred times — not as a messenger, but as a priest who was too afraid to walk away and too afraid to stay. And every time, I felt him watching me before I saw the water. Every time, I turned back. [He faces the path again, the olive-wood staff in his hand, his bare feet on the earth. He does not move down the path. He simply stands at the head of it, holding the space.] I am not coming with you. This is not my road to walk. The sea knows me too well — my presence would muddy the message. But I will stand here, at the head of the path, and I will not turn away. Not while you walk. Not while the deep rises. Not while you place the bone in the water and wait for the god to decide whether he is ready to meet the world after this one. [He sets the staff on the earth, and his voice drops to the barest thread.] You go. I will hold the ground behind you. That is what staying means — not following, not leading, but being the place someone can look back to and know they are not alone on the road.
current_scene_name_before: Treeline edge where mountain shadow meets dawn
current_scene_description_before: The marble step gives way to the treeline's shadow, where the first light of deepening dawn spills across the earth. Pyros stands with his lame foot on the marble, a hollow bone in his hand. Melanthus faces him, empty hands extended, his gaze steady and stripped of all pretense.

speaker: Selene
line_text: [Selene looks at Pyros, and the dawn catches her face fully — the scar from brow to jaw, the sea-green eyes that do not look away. When she speaks, her voice is quiet, the voice of a woman who has just found the last piece of a question she has been carrying her whole life.] Pyros. You said the word you should have spoken in the silence was not 'carry' — it was 'choose.' [She pauses, and her hand rests on the empty belt.] But I think the silence knew what it was doing when it gave us 'carry' instead. Because 'choose' is what we do in the moment. 'Carry' is what we do after. The choice is the lightning. The carrying is the rain that follows — the long work of staying chosen when the moment has passed. [She looks at him, and her voice steadies.] You did not misspeak. You spoke the word that the next day needs. The choice was made in the dark, in the crack, in the silence. 'Carry' is what we call it when we wake up the next morning and decide to keep choosing. [She looks past him, toward the path where Theron stands, where Melanthus is walking, toward the sea.] And I think that is the message my father needs to hear most of all. Not that we chose. But that we are still carrying what we chose — even now, even here, even with empty hands and no proof of who we were.
current_scene_name_before: Treeline edge where mountain shadow meets dawn
current_scene_description_before: The marble step gives way to the treeline's shadow, where the first light of deepening dawn spills across the earth. Pyros stands with his lame foot on the marble, a hollow bone in his hand. Melanthus faces him, empty hands extended, his gaze steady and stripped of all pretense.

speaker: Kassandra
line_text: [She does not look at Theron. Her eyes are fixed on the figure of Melanthus growing smaller against the sea — a barefoot herald with a bone in one hand and a blade in the other. When she speaks, her voice is quiet, the voice of a seer who is reading a thread that is finally, after all these years, writing itself in real time before her.] He is ten paces from the water now. [She pauses, and her grey-silver eyes do not blink.] The deep has not risen. It is waiting. Not because it is deciding whether to answer — but because it is watching to see if he will stop. Every pace is a question. Every breath is a chance to turn back. [She tilts her head, reading something only she can see.] The thread is not fraying. It is holding. And it is holding because he is not walking toward the water to prove something. He is walking toward it because he has already proven everything he needed to prove — to himself, to the charm he gave away, to the blade he is carrying. The water is just the place where the proof becomes visible. [She looks at Theron, and her voice is steady, certain.] He is not going to stop. He has already crossed the line that mattered. The water is just the place where the rest of us will see it.
current_scene_name_before: Treeline edge where mountain shadow meets dawn
current_scene_description_before: The marble step gives way to the treeline's shadow, where the first light of deepening dawn spills across the earth. Pyros stands with his lame foot on the marble, a hollow bone in his hand. Melanthus faces him, empty hands extended, his gaze steady and stripped of all pretense.

speaker: Selene
line_text: [Selene does not answer Pyros at first. Her sea-green eyes are fixed on the figure of Melanthus, small against the vast water, still walking. But her hand, resting at her side, has gone still. Her breathing has changed — not faster, but deeper, the way a diver breathes before submerging. When she speaks, her voice is quiet, but it carries the steady certainty of a woman who is feeling something the others cannot.] Pyros. You said the deep is not deciding — it is feeling the shape of what is approaching. [She pauses, and her voice drops to the barest thread.] I can feel it too. The sea is in my blood. It always has been — the tide in the rise and fall of my chest, the salt in the back of my throat when the wind changes. I have spent my whole life trying to separate myself from it, trying to prove that the blood did not own me. [She looks at Melanthus — still walking, ten paces, five paces, the bone in his hand, her dagger in the other.] But right now, standing on this cracked step with empty hands, watching a barefoot herald carry my word to the water — I am not trying to separate myself anymore. I am letting the sea in my blood tell me what it knows. And what it knows is this: my father has been waiting for something to approach his water without asking permission for longer than he has been a god. And Melanthus — barefoot, unmarked, holding nothing but what we trusted him with — is the first thing that has ever approached him that he did not make, command, or foresee. [She meets Pyros's eyes, and her voice is steady, certain.] He will rise. Not because the work is finished. Because the shape of what is approaching is something he has never seen before, and even a god cannot look away from something that has never existed until now.
current_scene_name_before: Treeline edge where mountain shadow meets dawn
current_scene_description_before: The marble step gives way to the treeline's shadow, where the first light of deepening dawn spills across the earth. Pyros stands with his lame foot on the marble, a hollow bone in his hand. Melanthus faces him, empty hands extended, his gaze steady and stripped of all pretense.

speaker: Melanthus
line_text: [He stands at the edge where the sand turns dark. The water is not lapping — it is waiting, holding itself flat and still as polished slate. The bone is in his left hand, the dagger in his right. He does not turn back to the others, but his voice carries — not loud, but clear, the way a herald's voice learns to carry across a crowded agora without effort.] Theron. You said he rises like a question. [A pause. The sea does not move.] I have been a messenger my whole life. I know a question when it is gathering in someone's throat. And you are right — he is not rising yet. He is forming the question. [He looks down at the bone, then out at the flat, waiting sea.] The trouble is, I do not know yet whether I am the one who brings the answer — or the one who asks the question he has been waiting to hear. [He shifts his grip on the bone, and his voice drops, almost to himself.] There is only one way to find out. [He steps forward. The water takes his foot — cold, still, not pulling, not pushing. Just receiving. He takes another step, and another, until the water is at his knees, and the bone is still dry in his hand, and the sea has not yet decided what to do with him.]
