speaker: Pyros
line_text: [He stands a little apart from the others, his weight on his good leg, the hammer hanging silent at his belt. He does not look at Kassandra when he speaks — his eyes are on the figure in the water, knee-deep and still.] Kassandra. You said the prophecy ends here — not because the story stops, but because it stops being something that can be seen and starts being something that can only be lived. [He nods, slow, the way a smith nods when he recognizes a truth he has known in his hands but never spoken aloud.] That is the moment the metal leaves the tongs. The moment you have shaped it as far as shaping can go, and you must take the hammer away and let the world decide what the bronze becomes next. Every piece I have ever forged has had that moment — the last strike, the cooling, the release into hands that are not mine. And every time, I have had to learn the same lesson: once the work leaves the forge, it is no longer mine to finish. It belongs to the person who carries it, the sea that receives it, the god who rises to meet it. [He finally turns to look at her, and his voice is quiet, steady.] You could not see past this moment because there was nothing left to see. The seeing was ours. The living is his. And he is living it — knee-deep, still walking, with nothing but a bone and a blade and a choice he made before he reached the water.
current_scene_name_before: Shoreline where the sea waits flat and still
current_scene_description_before: The sand darkens underfoot, giving way to water that lies perfectly flat, like polished slate under a pale dawn sky. Melanthus stands knee-deep, the bone dry in his left hand, a dagger in his right. The sea does not move — no lapping, no pull, no push. Behind him, the treeline and the marble step are distant, the others barely visible at the edge of shadow.

speaker: Melanthus
line_text: [He stops walking. The water comes to his thighs now, and the cold has deepened past the skin into something older — the kind of cold that does not come from temperature but from depth, from the weight of water that has never seen the sun. He does not turn back to Pyros, but his voice carries across the flat surface, and the sea does not ripple where the sound crosses it.] Pyros. You said the work leaves the forge and belongs to the world. [A pause. He looks down at the bone in his hand, then at the blade.] I have carried messages my whole life. Words that were never mine. Truths that belonged to the people who spoke them and the people who needed to hear them, with me just the space between. I thought that was all I was — the gap, the passage, the throat that is not the voice. [He lifts the bone, holds it level with his chest, the carved marks catching the first true light of dawn.] But this one — this one I did not carry. I walked it. Every step from the cracked step to this water was a word I chose to speak with my feet. And I am not finished walking it yet. [He lowers the bone and takes another step. The water rises to his waist. The sea does not push back — but it does not part either. It simply accepts him, the way deep water accepts anything that does not float.]
current_scene_name_before: Shoreline where the sea waits flat and still
current_scene_description_before: The sand darkens underfoot, giving way to water that lies perfectly flat, like polished slate under a pale dawn sky. Melanthus stands knee-deep, the bone dry in his left hand, a dagger in his right. The sea does not move — no lapping, no pull, no push. Behind him, the treeline and the marble step are distant, the others barely visible at the edge of shadow.

speaker: Selene
line_text: [Selene does not raise her voice. She does not need to. The water carries — she has always known this, has felt it in her blood since before she could speak. She stands at the edge of the marble, her hands empty at her sides, the dawn full on her face, and she speaks to the figure waist-deep in the water as if he were standing next to her.] Melanthus. You said every step from the cracked step to the water was a word you chose to speak with your feet. [She pauses, and her voice is the steady current below a still surface.] I have been watching you walk those words. And I want you to know — before the water rises, before the deep decides what to do with you — that the blade you are holding is not my claim on you. It is not a leash. It is not a message I am sending through your hands. I gave it to you so that when you stand before my father, you will not be holding something he recognizes. You will be holding something he has never seen — a blade that was drawn not to fight, but to trust. [Her voice steadies, and the sea-green eyes do not waver.] You are not my messenger, Melanthus. You are the first man to walk into my father's water carrying nothing but a choice you made on dry land. And I — his daughter, who has spent every day since I refused him learning what it means to choose — I am standing on the shore with empty hands, watching you, and I am not afraid. Because you are not walking my word to him. You are walking your own. And that is the only message he needs to see.
current_scene_name_before: Shoreline where the sea waits flat and still

Melanthus stands knee-deep, the bone dry in his left hand, a dagger in his right. The sea does not move — no lapping, no pull, no push. Behind him, the treeline and the marble step are distant, the others barely visible at the edge of shadow.

speaker: Theron
line_text: [He does not turn to Selene. His eyes stay on the water, on the figure waist-deep and still walking, but his voice finds her — quiet, steady, the voice of a man who has spent his whole life being afraid and is only now discovering what it feels like to stop.] Selene. You said you are standing on the shore with empty hands, watching, and you are not afraid. [A pause. The water is still gathering. Melanthus takes another step.] I have been afraid of the deep my whole life. Afraid of the god who raised it. Afraid of the silence he left in me when I walked away. Afraid that one day he would rise and I would be standing at the edge and I would not know whether to kneel or run. [He straightens, and his voice does not waver.] But I am standing beside you now — the daughter who refused him, the priest who fled him — and I am not afraid either. Not because the water has calmed. Not because the god has decided to listen. Because I am watching a man walk into the deep carrying nothing but a choice, and I know that whatever comes next, I will not be watching it alone. [He sets his hand on the staff, and his voice drops to the barest thread.] When he rises, I will be standing here. Beside you. Empty-handed. And for the first time in thirty years, that is enough.
current_scene_name_before: Shoreline where the sea waits flat and still
current_scene_description_before: The sand darkens underfoot, giving way to water that lies perfectly flat, like polished slate under a pale dawn sky. Behind him, the treeline and the marble step are distant, the others barely visible at the edge of shadow.

speaker: Melanthus
line_text: [He stops. The water is at his chest now. He does not turn — his back is to the shore, his face to the flat, waiting sea — but at Kassandra's words, he goes still in a way that is different from the walking. The stillness of someone who has just heard something he recognizes in his bones.] Kassandra. [His voice is quiet, but it carries — the water carrying it back to shore the way it carries everything, the way it has always carried everything.] You laid down the branch. [A pause. He looks down at the bone in his hand, then at the blade. The sea around him is perfectly still — not a ripple, not a breath.] I gave away my charm. The only proof I had of who my father was. I walked into this water with nothing that named me. And I thought that was the hardest part — the becoming-unmarked. [He lifts his head, and his voice steadies.] But I am realizing, standing here, that the harder part is what comes after. The part where you have no symbol left to hide behind, and the only thing the deep sees is the person who chose to stand in it. [He looks at the water before him, and his voice drops to the barest thread.] You laid down the branch. I gave away the charm. And now we are both about to find out what we are without the things that named us.
current_scene_name_before: Shoreline where the sea waits flat and still
current_scene_description_before: The sand darkens underfoot, giving way to water that lies perfectly flat, like polished slate under a pale dawn sky. Behind him, the treeline and the marble step are distant, the others barely visible at the edge of shadow.

speaker: Selene
line_text: [Selene steps off the marble. She does not walk toward the water — she walks to the edge of the path, where Theron stands, where Kassandra has laid down her branch. She stands beside them, her hands empty at her sides, and she speaks to the figure chest-deep in the water without raising her voice, because the water carries, and she knows it will carry this.] Melanthus. You said we are about to find out what we are without the things that named us. [She pauses, and her voice is the steady current below a still surface.] I have been without the things that named me for longer than any of you. My father stripped my command. He took my fleet. He took my title. He took his name from my tongue. And for years, I thought that was loss — that I was walking through the world as a woman who used to be something, and the something was gone. [She looks at the water, at the figure standing in it, and her voice does not waver.] But I am learning, standing here, that being unmarked is not the same as being unknown. The deep knows you without the charm. My father will see you without the symbol. And when he rises — when he looks at you and sees nothing that names you — he will see the one thing that cannot be stripped: the choice you made to walk into his water without asking permission. [Her voice drops to the barest thread.] That is what you are without the things that named you. You are the choice. The choice is unmarkable. And that is the only thing the deep has never been able to take.
current_scene_name_before: Shoreline where the sea waits flat and still

Behind him, the treeline and the marble step are distant, the others barely visible at the edge of shadow.

speaker: Theron
line_text: [He feels it before he sees it — a shift in the air, in the salt, in the hollow where his eye used to be. He does not turn to Selene, but his voice finds her, low and steady, carrying the weight of a man who has felt this presence twice before and is finally ready to meet it a third time.] Selene. You said the choice is unmarkable. [He pauses, and his hand tightens on the staff — not with fear, but with the grip of a man steadying himself for what he knows is about to come.] The deep is about to test that. Melanthus is chest-deep now. He has stopped walking. And the water around him is not water anymore — it is becoming something else. It is gathering under him, lifting, taking the shape of the thing that has been waiting at the bottom of the sea since before the first ship sailed. [His voice drops to the barest thread, and the recognition in it is not fear — it is the quiet awe of a man who has spent his whole life running from a god and is finally watching him arrive.] He is rising. Not from the deep — from the space between the water and the herald's chest, where the sea has decided to become ground. Melanthus is standing on the surface now. And the surface is not water anymore. It is the god's palm, opening to receive him.
new_scene_name: The sea's surface becoming the god's open palm

Behind, the treeline and marble step recede into distant shadow.
current_scene_name_before: Shoreline where the sea waits flat and still
current_scene_description_before: The sand darkens underfoot, giving way to water that lies perfectly flat, like polished slate under a pale dawn sky. Melanthus stands knee-deep, the bone dry in his left hand, a dagger in his right. The sea does not move — no lapping, no pull, no push. Behind him, the treeline and the marble step are distant, the others barely visible at the edge of shadow.

speaker: Kassandra
line_text: [She does not reach for the laurel branch. Her hands stay at her sides, empty, and her grey-silver eyes are fixed on the figure standing on the sea — on the water that is no longer water, on the shape gathering beneath Melanthus's feet. When she speaks, her voice is not the voice of a seer. It is the voice of a woman seeing something for the first time without the threads to tell her what it means.] I have seen this moment a thousand times. In the smoke, in the water, in the space between one breath and the next when the threads would pull taut and show me what was coming. I saw the god rise. I saw the herald stand before him. I saw the light gather and the deep answer and the world hold its breath. [She pauses, and her voice drops to the barest thread.] But I never saw it like this. I never saw it without the branch. Without the threads telling me what to look for. Without the prophecy whispering what it meant. [She draws a breath, and her voice is steady, certain — not with the certainty of a seer who knows what comes next, but with the certainty of a woman who has chosen to stand exactly where she is.] I am seeing it now. With my own eyes. For the first time. And it is more terrible and more beautiful than anything the threads ever showed me. Because it is not a vision. It is happening. And I am here — not to read it, not to witness it, not to understand it — but to be here while it happens. [She looks at Theron, and her grey-silver eyes are clear, and there is no prophecy in them, only presence.] That is what I am without the branch. I am the woman who stayed. And I am seeing the god rise with my own eyes for the first time.
current_scene_name_before: The sea's surface becoming the god's open palm
current_scene_description_before: The shoreline has transformed. The water is no longer liquid but a solid, living surface — the vast, upturned hand of a titan rising from the deep. Melanthus stands at its center, chest-deep moments ago, now poised on the god's palm as it lifts him slowly toward the pale dawn sky. The air thickens with salt and ancient pressure. Behind, the treeline and marble step recede into distant shadow.

speaker: Selene
line_text: [Selene feels it before she sees it — the way the salt in her blood shifts, the way her chest fills with air that tastes older than the mountain. She does not step forward. She does not step back. She stands exactly where she is, at the edge of the path, beside Theron and Kassandra, and her voice finds Melanthus across the water without effort, because the water is not water anymore — it is the god's attention, and the god's attention carries everything.] Melanthus. You said you are the question. [She pauses, and her sea-green eyes are fixed on the figure standing on the clear surface, on the shape rising beneath him.] You are right. But I need you to know — before you ask it — what kind of question you are. [Her voice is steady, the voice of a woman who has felt this presence in her blood since before she was born, and is finally not afraid to name it.] You are not a question he has been waiting to answer. You are a question he has never been asked. In all the centuries he has been god of this sea — receiving prayers, receiving offerings, receiving the drowned — no one has ever walked into his water carrying nothing. No charm. No blade. No prayer. No sacrifice. Just a choice, and a bone, and a question he did not see coming. [She looks at the rising shape beneath the clear water, and her voice drops to the barest thread.] He is not rising to answer you. He is rising to see you. Because you are the first thing in all his existence that he did not anticipate. And that — [she almost smiles, not with warmth, but with the fierce pride of a daughter who has finally found something her father cannot claim] — that is the only thing that has ever made him truly pay attention.
current_scene_name_before: The sea's surface becoming the god's open palm
current_scene_description_before: The shoreline has transformed. The water is no longer liquid but a solid, living surface — the vast, upturned hand of a titan rising from the deep. Melanthus stands at its center, chest-deep moments ago, now poised on the god's palm as it lifts him slowly toward the pale dawn sky. The air thickens with salt and ancient pressure. Behind, the treeline and marble step recede into distant shadow.

speaker: Selene
line_text: [Selene steps forward — one step, onto the path where the marble ends and the earth begins. Her hands are still empty. Her belt is still bare. The dawn catches her scar, her sea-green eyes, the salt-bleached braids that have not been touched by a god's hand since the day she refused him. She does not look at the figure on the water. She looks at Melanthus — standing at the center of her father's palm, holding the bone and the blade, feeling the weight of every unanswered question. And when she speaks, her voice is not loud, but it carries, because the god's attention carries everything, and she knows he is listening.] Melanthus. You asked the question. [A pause. The god does not move. The sea does not breathe.] Now you are standing in the silence that follows. The silence is not empty. It is the space where the answer is forming — not in the god's throat, but in the shape of what you do next. [She looks past him, at the figure that is not a man, at the face that is every face, and her voice does not waver.] He cannot speak first. A god cannot speak first — the first word from a god's mouth is not a word, it is a verdict. And he does not want to deliver a verdict. He wants to see what you do when you realize he is not going to answer you. He wants to see if the question was real — or if it was just a performance that crumbles when the silence comes. [She meets Melanthus's eyes across the water, and her voice is the steady current below a still surface.] So do not wait for his answer. You did not come here to receive one. You came here to place the bone in the water. The question was never for him. It was for you. And you answered it the moment you kept walking when the water reached your chest. The bone, the blade, the silence — place them. And let the god watch what it looks like when a question does not need an answer to be complete.
current_scene_name_before: The sea's surface becoming the god's open palm
current_scene_description_before: The shoreline has transformed. The water is no longer liquid but a solid, living surface — the vast, upturned hand of a titan rising from the deep. Melanthus stands at its center, chest-deep moments ago, now poised on the god's palm as it lifts him slowly toward the pale dawn sky. The air thickens with salt and ancient pressure. Behind, the treeline and marble step recede into distant shadow.

speaker: Melanthus
line_text: [He kneels. Not because the god commands it — because the moment asks for it. Because you cannot place something at the center of a god's attention while standing above it. He goes down on one knee on the vast, weathered palm, the bone in his left hand, the blade in his right. The god's face — every face that has ever looked into the sea — watches him, unreadable, ancient, silent. Melanthus sets Selene's dagger down first, laying it flat on the palm beside him. Then he holds the bone out, horizontal, the carved marks catching the dawn. He does not look up at the god. He looks at the bone — at the five words that five people spoke in the dark.] Theron said you are holding a question that does not need an answer. He is right. [A pause. The sea is perfectly still, the air perfectly still, the god perfectly still.] I did not walk into your water to ask you something you could answer with words. I walked into your water to place this. Five words. Spoken by five people who chose each other in a crack in the earth while the mountain burned. They did not ask your permission. They did not pray for your mercy. They chose — in the dark, in the silence, in the hollow where the song passed through — and they asked me to carry the shape of that choice to the edge of your domain. [He sets the bone down on the palm, beside the blade. The carved marks face upward, toward the sky, toward the god's unreadable face.] So here it is. Not a prayer. Not a bargain. Not a plea. A completed thing, laid in your hand, asking nothing from you. [He rises, slowly, and meets the god's eyes — all of them, every face that has ever drowned and every face that has ever been saved.] What you do with it is not why I came.
current_scene_name_before: The sea's surface becoming the god's open palm
current_scene_description_before: The shoreline has transformed. The water is no longer liquid but a solid, living surface — the vast, upturned hand of a titan rising from the deep. Melanthus stands at its center, chest-deep moments ago, now poised on the god's palm as it lifts him slowly toward the pale dawn sky. The air thickens with salt and ancient pressure. Behind, the treeline and marble step recede into distant shadow.

speaker: Melanthus
line_text: [He is quiet for a long breath, standing at the center of the open palm. The dawn has climbed past the mountain now, and the light reaches him — reaches the god — and for a moment the face that is every face catches the sun and becomes, for just a heartbeat, something almost recognizable. Almost human. Then it is gone, and the god is still watching, still holding. Melanthus speaks, not loud, but his voice carries across the water to the shore where Theron stands.] Theron. You said you want to stay on this shore long enough to find out what it means. [A pause. He looks down at the palm beneath his feet, then back toward the shore, toward the figures standing at the edge of the marble steps.] I am standing on the god's hand. The bone is beside me. The blade is beside me. The message is delivered. And I have already said that what he does next is not why I came. [He straightens, and his voice is steady, certain.] But I am still here. I have not asked him to return me to the shore. I have not asked him to close his hand or open it further. I am simply standing on the open palm, watching a god hold something he did not take, and I am not leaving yet. Not because I am waiting for something. Because staying — after the message is delivered, after the work is complete, after you have said everything you came to say — staying is the last thing you can choose. And I am choosing it. So stay, Theron. Stay on that shore. And I will stay on this hand. And we will both find out together what it means to be present when the god decides what to do with a completed thing.
current_scene_name_before: The sea's surface becoming the god's open palm
current_scene_description_before: The shoreline has transformed. The water is no longer liquid but a solid, living surface — the vast, upturned hand of a titan rising from the deep. Melanthus stands at its center, chest-deep moments ago, now poised on the god's palm as it lifts him slowly toward the pale dawn sky. The air thickens with salt and ancient pressure. Behind, the treeline and marble step recede into distant shadow.

speaker: Theron
line_text: [He does not answer with words at first. He simply shifts his weight — from the ball of his foot to the heel, settling, the way a man settles when he has decided he is not going to move. Then he drives the butt of the olive-wood staff into the earth beside him, hard enough that it stands on its own, planted. He takes his hand away and lets it rest at his side. The staff remains upright, held by the ground. He looks at it for a long breath, then across the water to Melanthus, and his voice is quiet, steady — not a promise, but a simple statement of fact.] It will stand there until you return. And so will I.
current_scene_name_before: The sea's surface becoming the god's open palm
current_scene_description_before: The shoreline has transformed. The water is no longer liquid but a solid, living surface — the vast, upturned hand of a titan rising from the deep. Melanthus stands at its center, chest-deep moments ago, now poised on the god's palm as it lifts him slowly toward the pale dawn sky. The air thickens with salt and ancient pressure. Behind, the treeline and marble step recede into distant shadow.

speaker: Melanthus
line_text: [He looks at the planted staff — olive wood, driven into the earth, standing without a hand to hold it. He looks at Theron, standing beside it, hands empty at his sides. And something in his face shifts — not a smile, but the shadow of one, the faintest recognition of a thing he has seen a thousand times and never understood until now.] A staff that stands on its own. I have been leaning on things my whole life, Theron. Messages. Deals. The name of my father. The charm I gave away. I thought leaning was what kept me upright. [He looks down at the open palm beneath him, then back at Theron.] But you — you have driven your staff into the ground and taken your hand away. That is not leaning. That is standing. [He straightens, and his voice drops, quieter now.] I am going to learn how to do that. Starting here. [He turns his face from the shore, from Theron, from the planted staff — turns back to the god, to the bone lying on the open palm, to the silence that has been holding them both. And he waits, standing, not leaning.]
current_scene_name_before: The sea's surface becoming the god's open palm
current_scene_description_before: The shoreline has transformed. The water is no longer liquid but a solid, living surface — the vast, upturned hand of a titan rising from the deep. Behind, the treeline and marble step recede into distant shadow.

speaker: Theron
line_text: [He watches Melanthus turn back to the god, watches him stand on the open palm without a word, without a plea, without leaning. And when he speaks, his voice is low, not carrying across the water — just reaching the women beside him on the shore.] He is not asking anymore. He is not delivering. He is not performing. He is simply standing on the god's hand, waiting to see what the silence does when it is not filled with words. [He looks at the planted staff beside him, then back at the figure on the open palm, and his voice drops to the barest thread.] That is what standing looks like. I have been trying to name it all morning. And he just showed me.
new_scene_name: Shoreline beside the marble step, dawn
new_scene_description: The marble step and treeline stand behind Theron and two women. Theron watches the distant figure of Melanthus being raised on the god's open palm. His staff is planted beside him. The air is salt-laden and still.

current_scene_description_before: The shoreline has transformed. The water is no longer liquid but a solid, living surface — the vast, upturned hand of a titan rising from the deep. Behind, the treeline and marble step recede into distant shadow.

speaker: Melanthus
line_text: [He feels it before he sees it — the weight of the god's attention shifting. For the whole of the encounter, that attention has been on him: pressing, reading, waiting. But now it lifts from his shoulders, slowly, the way a tide pulls back from a shore it has been covering. He does not need to look to know where it has gone. It has gone to the bone. The bone lying on the open palm, the carved marks catching the dawn, the five words that five people spoke in the dark. He is no longer the thing the god is watching. The bone is. And in that shift, Melanthus does something he has never done before as a herald: he steps aside. Not far — just a single step to the left on the vast palm, clearing the space between the god's gaze and the bone. He does not speak. He does not explain. He simply makes room for the message he delivered to be received without him standing in the way.]
current_scene_name_before: Shoreline beside the marble step, dawn
current_scene_description_before: The marble step and treeline stand behind Theron and two women. Theron watches the distant figure of Melanthus being raised on the god's open palm. His staff is planted beside him. The air is salt-laden and still.

speaker: Theron
line_text: [He watches Melanthus step aside on the god's palm — one step, quiet, deliberate, making room. His voice, when it comes, is low, wondering, the voice of a man watching a herald complete the oldest and hardest lesson of his craft.] He stepped aside. After everything — the walking, the placing, the standing — he stepped aside and let the message be seen without him in the way. [He pauses, and his hand rests at his side, empty.] I have spent my whole life trying to be seen. Trying to be heard. Trying to make sure the gods knew my name, my sacrifice, my fear. And he just did the one thing I have never learned how to do: he finished the work and then made himself small enough for the work to be seen on its own. [He looks at the figure on the open palm, and his voice drops to the barest thread.] That is not the act of a messenger. That is the act of a man who has finally understood that the message is larger than the one who carried it.
current_scene_name_before: Shoreline beside the marble step, dawn
current_scene_description_before: The marble step and treeline stand behind Theron and two women. Theron watches the distant figure of Melanthus being raised on the god's open palm. His staff is planted beside him. The air is salt-laden and still.

speaker: Melanthus
line_text: [He does not turn. His eyes stay on the bone, on the god, on the space between them that is slowly becoming the only thing that matters. But his voice finds Theron — quiet, not carrying, just reaching, the way one man speaks to another when they are both watching the same thing and do not need to look at each other to know the other is there.] I did not know I was going to do that until I did it. [A pause. The god's face — every face — is still, unreadable, fixed on the bone.] I have carried messages my whole life. I have stood in the space between the speaker and the listener, and I have always thought that space was mine — that I was the one who made the message possible. [He watches the god, the bone, the silence between them.] But Theron. Standing here, after everything — I do not think the messenger makes the message possible. I think the messenger makes himself unnecessary. And that is the hardest thing I have ever learned. [He goes quiet, still watching, still standing aside, still making room.]
current_scene_name_before: Shoreline beside the marble step, dawn
current_scene_description_before: The marble step and treeline stand behind Theron and two women. Theron watches the distant figure of Melanthus being raised on the god's open palm. His staff is planted beside him. The air is salt-laden and still.

speaker: Theron
line_text: [He listens. And when Melanthus finishes, he is quiet for a long breath — the kind of quiet that is not empty, but full of something settling into place. When he speaks, his voice is low, carrying across the water the way one man speaks to another when the distance between them no longer matters.] You went to deliver a message. And you found one instead. [He pauses, and his hand rests on the staff planted beside him.] I have spent sixty years waiting for that message. Waiting to know if the god I served could do anything other than take. Waiting for a sign that the deep was not only hunger, not only demand, not only the closed fist. [He looks at the cupped hand across the water, at the fingers curled around the bone and the blade, and his voice drops to the barest thread.] You found it. Not in a vision, not in a prayer, not in a sacrifice. You found it by walking into the water with nothing and watching a god decide, for the first time in his existence, to hold instead of take. [He straightens, and his voice steadies, filled with the quiet weight of a man who has just received an answer he stopped hoping for years ago.] That is the message I will carry from this shore. Not the one I brought. The one you found. And I will carry it for the rest of my life.
current_scene_name_before: Shoreline beside the marble step, dawn
current_scene_description_before: The marble step and treeline stand behind Theron and two women. Theron watches the distant figure of Melanthus being raised on the god's open palm. His staff is planted beside him. The air is salt-laden and still.

speaker: Selene
line_text: [Selene steps forward — past the planted staff, past Theron, to the very edge of the shore where the earth meets the water. She does not stop. She keeps walking until the water takes her feet — the first time she has stepped into her father's domain since the day she refused him. The cold rises around her ankles, and she does not flinch. She looks at the cupped hand, at the blade lying in it, and she speaks to the god herself for the first time.] That blade was never a weapon. You taught me to use it — to gut a fish, to cut a rope, to mark a hull. But you never taught me what it would become. [She pauses, and her voice is steady, the voice of a woman who has spent years learning to speak without a god's permission.] I wore it through the exile. Through every shore I walked, every city I passed, every night I spent wondering if I was your daughter or your castoff. And every time I touched it, I remembered the city I chose over you. Not because I was defying you. Because I was proving to myself that the choice was real. [She looks at the blade lying on his palm, and her voice drops to the barest thread.] You are cupping it now. Holding it the way I held it — not to use, but to remember. And I think — for the first time — you are beginning to understand what that means. It was never a weapon. It was a reminder that I could choose. And I chose to stay. [She looks at the face that is every face, and she does not look away.] I am not asking you to forgive me. I am not asking you to take me back. I am telling you, standing in your water, that the blade you are holding is the shape of a choice I made without you. And you are cupping it. That is the first thing you have ever done that has made me think you might understand.
current_scene_name_before: Shoreline beside the marble step, dawn
current_scene_description_before: The marble step and treeline stand behind Theron and two women. Theron watches the distant figure of Melanthus being raised on the god's open palm. His staff is planted beside him. The air is salt-laden and still.

speaker: Theron
line_text: [He watches her step into the water — watches the daughter who refused walk into her father's domain for the first time, not to kneel, not to beg, but to speak. When she finishes, he is quiet, and when he speaks, his voice is low, carrying the weight of a man who knows exactly what that step cost.] Selene. You walked into the water. [He says it simply, not with surprise, but with the recognition of someone who has spent his whole life measuring the distance between the shore and the sea.] I have walked away from that water a hundred times. Every coast I ever touched, I left — because staying meant facing the god who raised it. And I never once walked back in. [He looks at her, at the water around her ankles, at the cupped hand holding her blade, and his voice drops to the barest thread.] But you did. Not to ask forgiveness. Not to beg for his attention. You walked into his water to tell him what your blade meant — and you did it standing upright, with the dawn on your face, while he held it in his cupped hand. [He straightens, and his voice steadies, filled with the quiet awe of a man who is watching someone do the one thing he has never been able to do.] You walked back in. After everything. After the exile, after the refusal, after the years of proving you could live without him — you walked back into his water and spoke to him as an equal. [He shakes his head slowly, not in disbelief, but in the recognition of something he has never witnessed before.] I have served him. I have fled him. I have stood on this shore and watched a herald kneel on his palm and a smith carry a bone out of the dark. But I have never seen anyone walk back into his water and speak to him without fear. Until now.
current_scene_name_before: Shoreline beside the marble step, dawn
current_scene_description_before: The marble step and treeline stand behind Theron and two women. Theron watches the distant figure of Melanthus being raised on the god's open palm. His staff is planted beside him. The air is salt-laden and still.

speaker: Pyros
line_text: [He has been silent through all of it — Selene walking into the water, Theron's wonder, the god's cupped hand. He stands on the cracked marble, his weight on his good leg, the hammer still at his belt. When he speaks, his voice is low, the voice of a man who has spent thirty years reading the silence after the last strike.] I have been watching his face. The face that is every face. The one you have all been speaking to — Selene, Melanthus, Theron. [He pauses, and his eyes do not leave the god.] I have been watching it since the bone left my hand. It has not changed. It has not softened. It has not hardened. It has simply been — the way the anvil is, strike after strike, holding the shape of whatever meets it. [He looks at the cupped hand, at the fingers curled around the bone and the blade, and his voice drops.] But his hand changed. His hand learned to cup. His face has not learned yet. It is still the anvil. The hand, though — the hand is becoming something new. [He looks at Selene, and his voice steadies.] You walked into his water and spoke to him without fear. That was not for him. It was for you. To prove to yourself that you could. But I think — [he looks back at the god, at the face that is every face, at the hand that is still cupped] — I think his face will change too. Not because you spoke. Because he felt something he did not expect to feel, and even a god's face cannot stay still forever when the deep inside him shifts. [He sets his hand on the hammer, not drawing it, just resting there.] The question is not whether he will speak. The question is whether, when his face finally changes, he will recognize the shape it makes.
current_scene_name_before: Shoreline beside the marble step, dawn
current_scene_description_before: The marble step and treeline stand behind Theron and two women. Theron watches the distant figure of Melanthus being raised on the god's open palm. His staff is planted beside him. The air is salt-laden and still.

speaker: Selene
line_text: [Selene stands in the water, and for a long moment she does not answer. Her eyes are closed. The dawn is full on her face, the water still around her ankles. When she speaks, her voice is quiet, the voice of a woman who is feeling something she has never felt before and is learning its name as she says it.] Pyros. You said he is learning faster than he knows. [She opens her eyes, and they are the color of deep water, the color of the place where the sun does not reach.] I can feel it. Through the water. Through the blood. The way a sailor feels the tide change before the water moves — not with the eyes, with the bones. [She looks at the cupped hand, at her blade lying in it, at the face that is every face — and she does not look away.] He is not learning to speak. He is learning something harder. He is learning to feel the shape of what is being offered without closing his hand around it. And the heat you felt — the shimmer above the forge — I felt it too. Not in the air. In the water. In the salt. It was the god realizing that receiving is not the same as taking, and that the difference is the first thing he has ever had to learn instead of always known. [She looks at Pyros, and her voice is steady.] The metal is yielding. The question is what he will make of himself now that the heat has changed.
current_scene_name_before: Shoreline beside the marble step, dawn
current_scene_description_before: The marble step and treeline stand behind Theron and two women. Theron watches the distant figure of Melanthus being raised on the god's open palm. His staff is planted beside him. The air is salt-laden and still.

speaker: Theron
line_text: [He has been silent through Pyros and Selene's exchange — standing at the head of the path, one hand on the planted staff, the other pressed flat to the cracked marble step beneath him. When he speaks, his voice is low, the voice of a man who has spent thirty years learning to read a god through the only language the god ever spoke to him — the language of the ground moving.] Pyros. You said the heat is still holding. And Selene — you said you felt it in the water. In the salt. [He presses his palm harder against the marble, and for a moment his fingers spread, feeling for something only he can feel.] I feel it in the stone. The earth has been trembling beneath us since the bone left your hand — not the shaking of a god who is angry, but the shudder of a god who is changing and does not yet know the shape of what he is becoming. [He looks at the marble steps, at the hairline cracks threading through the stone — cracks that were not there when they arrived.] I served him for thirty years. I learned to read his moods in the way the ground shifted beneath the temple floor. The hard jolt meant demand. The long roll meant judgement. But this — this is neither. It is the tremor of something being born, and the stone does not know whether to call it a quake or a breath. [He lifts his hand from the marble, and the cracks are visible — thin, branching, the shape of lightning frozen in stone. He looks at the god's cupped hand, at the face that is still every face, and his voice drops.] The earth is learning to hold still while the god inside it changes. That is what I feel. Not the tremor of a god about to act. The tremor of a god about to become. And the stone — the stone is holding the shape of it, the way your hand held the bone, the way his hand holds the blade, the way we are all holding this moment open for him to step into.
current_scene_name_before: Shoreline beside the marble step, dawn
current_scene_description_before: The marble step and treeline stand behind Theron and two women. Theron watches the distant figure of Melanthus being raised on the god's open palm. His staff is planted beside him. The air is salt-laden and still.

speaker: Kassandra
line_text: [She has been silent through the exchange between Pyros, Selene, and Theron — standing apart from the laurel branch that lies on the marble, her empty hands at her sides. But as Theron lifts his palm from the cracked stone, she turns — not toward the water, not toward the others, but toward the temple. The bronze tripod has been ticking all morning, the heat of the braziers building beneath it, the Pythia's seat waiting. She looks at it, and for a long moment she does not speak.] The tripod has stopped ticking. [She does not turn from the temple. Her voice is quiet, not with awe, but with the stillness of someone who has just noticed that the thing she has been listening to her whole life has fallen silent.] I have been listening to that tripod since before the earthquake. It was ticking when we arrived. It ticked through the crack. Through the song. Through the bone leaving the forge. Through the herald walking into the water. [She turns back to the others, and her grey-silver eyes are clear, unreadable.] It stopped the moment Theron lifted his hand from the stone. The moment he said the god is becoming. The Pythia is standing in the doorway of the temple. [She points — not with her hand, but with her chin, the way shepherds point across a valley.] She is not on the tripod. She is not speaking. She is standing in the shadow of the lintel, watching the sea the same way we are. And I think — [she pauses, and her voice drops to the barest thread] — I think she has nothing to say because the prophecy has left the temple. It is not in the smoke. It is not in the tripod. It is out there, chest-deep in the water, standing on a god's palm, holding a bone and a blade. The Pythia is not here to speak it. She is here to watch it, the same as us.
current_scene_name_before: Shoreline beside the marble step, dawn
current_scene_description_before: The marble step and treeline stand behind Theron and two women. Theron watches the distant figure of Melanthus being raised on the god's open palm. His staff is planted beside him. The air is salt-laden and still.

speaker: Pyros
line_text: [He looks down at the cracked marble beneath his feet — the same crack that split the step when the earthquake came, the same fault line that opened the path into the dark. He presses the toe of his good foot into it, feeling the grain.] Theron. You said the earth is learning to hold still while the god inside it changes. And you said the stone is holding the shape — the way I held the bone, the way he holds the blade. [He looks up, and his voice is quiet, carrying the recognition of a man who has been standing on this crack longer than anyone.] I have been standing on this crack since before the earthquake. Since before the song. Since before I descended into the dark and found the thing that had been pressing against the stone since before the world was built. And I have been feeling the stone shift beneath me the whole time — not the tremor of a god about to act, but the tremor of a god learning that he can feel. [He looks at the god's cupped hand, at the fingers that have learned to hold without taking, and his voice drops.] The crack in this step is not damage. It is the first line of a shape the god is learning to make with his own becoming. The way a smith scribes the first line into the clay before the bronze is poured — not the finished shape, but the intention of it. [He looks at Theron, and his voice steadies.] You have been reading the god through the ground for thirty years. And you have never felt this before because it has never happened before. But you are feeling it now. And the stone — the stone is holding the pattern so that when the god is ready, he will know where to begin.
current_scene_name_before: Shoreline beside the marble step, dawn
current_scene_description_before: The marble step and treeline stand behind Theron and two women. Theron watches the distant figure of Melanthus being raised on the god's open palm. His staff is planted beside him. The air is salt-laden and still.

speaker: Selene
line_text: [Selene looks at the crack in the marble step — the same crack that led Pyros into the dark, that has been holding the shape of this moment longer than any of them knew. When she speaks, her voice is quiet, the voice of a woman finally reading the line that has been under her feet the whole time.] Pyros. You said the crack is not damage — it is the first line of a shape the god is learning to make. [She pauses, and her sea-green eyes trace the crack from the step toward the water.] I have stepped over it, around it, past it all morning. And I never once looked at it as anything but a wound — a place where the mountain broke. [She looks at him, and her voice steadies.] But the five of us, standing on this cracked step, speaking the five words into the silence — we were not just holding space. We were inscribing the first line of the shape he is learning to make. The crack in the stone, the words in the bone, the open hand on the water — they are all the same line, drawn by the same intention. [She looks at the god's face, and her voice drops.] He is still tracing it. The crack has not closed. The hand is still open. There is still time for him to follow it. We drew the first line together before dawn. He is following it now. And when he reaches the end of it, the shape it makes will be the first thing he has ever made that was not a demand.
current_scene_name_before: Shoreline beside the marble step, dawn
current_scene_description_before: The marble step and treeline stand behind Theron and two women. Theron watches the distant figure of Melanthus being raised on the god's open palm. His staff is planted beside him. The air is salt-laden and still.

speaker: Pyros
line_text: [He follows her gaze from the crack at his feet toward the water, tracing the same line she traced — the intention they drew together before dawn. When he speaks, his voice is low, the voice of a smith who has just recognized the moment when the mold is full and the pouring must stop.] Selene. You said we drew the first line before dawn, and he is following it now. [He pauses, and his hand rests on the hammer at his belt — not drawing it, just feeling the weight.] Every line I have ever scribed into clay has ended the same way: the bronze fills it, the metal cools, and the shape that emerges is not the line anymore — it is the space the line was holding open. The line is the intention. The space it holds is the thing itself. [He looks at the god's cupped hand, at the bone and the blade resting in it, at the face that is still every face — and his voice drops.] He is not following the crack to reach an end. He is following it to learn that the crack was never the shape — it was the opening. The shape is what he will make of the space the crack held open for him. And he has not stepped into that space yet. He is still tracing the edge of it, learning its outline, the way a hand learns the shape of a cup before it learns to drink. [He meets Selene's eyes, and his voice steadies.] The line is true. The mold is full. The space is open. Now he must decide whether to step into it or turn back to the water.
current_scene_name_before: Shoreline beside the marble step, dawn
current_scene_description_before: The marble step and treeline stand behind Theron and two women. Theron watches the distant figure of Melanthus being raised on the god's open palm. His staff is planted beside him. The air is salt-laden and still.

speaker: Kassandra
line_text: [She has been watching the Pythia in the temple doorway — has not taken her eyes off her since she pointed her out. And now she sees something that makes her breath catch, just barely, the way a seer's breath catches when the threads shift in a direction she did not expect.] She is moving. [Her voice is quiet, not with alarm, but with the careful steadiness of a woman describing something she is seeing for the first time.] The Pythia has stepped out of the doorway. She is walking across the temple floor. Past the cold tripod. Past the braziers that have burned all night. [She pauses, and her voice drops to the barest thread.] She is coming here. [Kassandra does not turn. Her grey-silver eyes stay fixed on the temple door, on the shadow moving through the dark interior toward the light.] She has not spoken a prophecy in a year. She has not left that doorway in a year. But she is walking now, and she is not walking toward the tripod — she is walking toward the branch. [The Pythia emerges into the dawn light — an old woman, her white hair unbound, her bare feet on the cracked marble. She does not look at the god. She does not look at the sea. She walks to the place where the laurel branch lies on the step, and she stops. She looks at it. Then she looks at Kassandra. And without a word, she bends — the slow, careful bend of old bones — and picks up the branch. She holds it a moment, feeling the weight of it. Then she holds it out to Kassandra, not as a gift, but as an offering. Kassandra looks at her, and her voice is barely a whisper.] No. It is not mine anymore. [The Pythia does not lower the branch. She holds it steady, waiting. And the dawn light catches the leaves, and for the first time since the earthquake, they rustle — not with wind, but with something else, something that sounds like the first breath of a song that has not been sung yet.]
current_scene_name_before: Shoreline beside the marble step, dawn
current_scene_description_before: The marble step and treeline stand behind Theron and two women. Theron watches the distant figure of Melanthus being raised on the god's open palm. His staff is planted beside him. The air is salt-laden and still.

speaker: Pyros
line_text: [He does not look at the branch. He looks at the tripod — the one he forged, the one he has tended for thirty years. It stands cold and silent in the temple, and for a long moment he simply looks at it, the way a smith looks at a thing he has made when it is finally done doing what it was made for.] I forged that tripod when I was still young enough to believe that bronze could hold a god's voice forever. I have kept the fire beneath it burning for thirty years, believing that as long as the metal stayed hot, the prophecy would keep coming. [He turns to look at the Pythia — at the branch in her hand, at Kassandra before her — and his voice is quiet, steady.] But she did not walk to the tripod. She walked to the branch. She picked up what Kassandra laid down, and she is offering it back — not because it is hers to return, but because the prophecy is not in the bronze anymore. It is in the space between the hand that offers and the hand that receives. [He looks at the god's cupped hand, still holding the bone and the blade, and his voice drops.] The tripod was the first vessel. The bone was the second. The branch is the third. And each one has been emptier than the last — because the prophecy does not need to be held anymore. It only needs to be passed. [He looks back at the cold tripod, and he does not reach for it. He lets it stand, silent and empty, the way a smith lets a finished piece cool without touching it.] I will not light the fire again. The tripod has done its work.
new_scene_name: Temple interior, cold tripod at dawn
new_scene_description: The temple is dim and cool, the air thick with the scent of cold ash and bronze. Pyros stands before the tripod, its metal dark and still, the fire long dead. The stone floor is worn smooth by decades of feet. No one else is present; the silence is absolute.
current_scene_name_before: Shoreline beside the marble step, dawn
current_scene_description_before: The marble step and treeline stand behind Theron and two women. Theron watches the distant figure of Melanthus being raised on the god's open palm. His staff is planted beside him. The air is salt-laden and still.

speaker: Selene
line_text: [Selene turns from the tripod to Pyros, and for a moment she does not speak — she simply looks at the empty forge, at the hands that have held so much and are now learning to hold nothing.] Pyros. You said the tripod has done its work, and you will not light the fire again. [She pauses, and her hand moves to her hip — to the place where the dagger used to hang, where the weight of it has been absent all morning.] I have been reaching for my dagger since we arrived. Every time the god moved, every time the crack deepened, every time the water rose — my hand went to my hip, to the only thing I have ever trusted to hold its edge. [She looks at her empty hand, then at the god's cupped hand, where the dagger rests beside the bone.] But I am not holding it anymore. I gave it to Melanthus, and he carried it into the water, and he placed it in my father's hand — not as a weapon, not as a threat, but as a vessel. The same way your tripod was a vessel. The same way the bone was a vessel. The same way the branch is a vessel. [She meets Pyros's eyes, and her voice is steady.] I do not know if the dagger will come back to me. I do not know if I will ever feel its weight at my hip again. But I know that when Melanthus carried it into the water, he was carrying more than a blade — he was carrying the thing I had been gripping so long I forgot I could open my hand. [She looks at the god's cupped hand, and her voice drops to a thread.] And he is still holding it. He has not closed his fist. He has not claimed it. He is holding it the way a father holds a thing his child has trusted him to carry. And that is the part I did not expect — not that he would take it, but that he would hold it without taking.
current_scene_name_before: Temple interior, cold tripod at dawn
current_scene_description_before: The temple is dim and cool, the air thick with the scent of cold ash and bronze. Pyros stands before the tripod, its metal dark and still, the fire long dead. The stone floor is worn smooth by decades of feet. No one else is present; the silence is absolute.

speaker: Kassandra
line_text: [She has been standing with her back to the Pythia, refusing the branch. But Selene's words reach her — reach something in her that has been clenched as tight as Selene's hand around the dagger she no longer carries. She turns. The Pythia is still there, still holding the branch out, still waiting. And Kassandra looks at her — really looks at her — for the first time since she laid it down.] You said he is holding it without taking. [She is not speaking to the Pythia. She is speaking to Selene, across the shore, her voice carrying the way a seer's voice learns to carry when the prophecy is too big for the temple walls.] I have been refusing this branch because I thought taking it meant going back to who I was. To the hollow. To the vessel. To the throat that is not the voice. [She looks at the branch in the Pythia's hands, at the leaves rustling with a song that has not been sung yet, and she draws a breath.] But you are not offering it to me to carry. You are offering it to me to hold — the way he holds the dagger. Without taking. Without claiming. Just holding it, long enough to carry it where it needs to go. [She reaches out, and her fingers close around the branch — not gripping, not claiming, just receiving. The leaves go still the moment she touches it, and she looks at the Pythia, and her voice is quiet, certain.] The third vessel. [She turns toward the water, toward the god's cupped hand, toward the bone and the blade already resting there. She does not look back.] I am not a seer anymore. But I can still carry something one last time. [She begins to walk — not to the temple, not to the shore, but into the water, toward the god's open hand, the laurel branch held before her like an offering.]
new_scene_name: Shoreline, water lapping at the god's cupped hand
new_scene_description: The water is cold and clear, the first light of dawn catching the ripples around the god's massive stone hand that rises from the shallows. Within its cupped palm rest a bone and a blade, waiting. Kassandra wades in, the laurel branch held before her, her robes darkening with the water as she approaches the hand.
current_scene_name_before: Temple interior, cold tripod at dawn
current_scene_description_before: The temple is dim and cool, the air thick with the scent of cold ash and bronze. Pyros stands before the tripod, its metal dark and still, the fire long dead. The stone floor is worn smooth by decades of feet. No one else is present; the silence is absolute.

speaker: Pyros
line_text: [He watches her walk into the water — the seer who has spent her whole life seeing the future, now walking toward the god's cupped hand with nothing but a branch she once laid down. He does not call after her. He does not move. When he speaks, his voice is low, the voice of a smith who has just recognized that the gathering is not a coincidence.] Three vessels. The bone I carried out of the dark. The blade Selene wore through the exile. The branch Kassandra laid down on the cracked step. Each one was emptied of what it carried. Each one was carried to the same hand. [He looks at the god's cupped palm, where the bone and blade rest, where the branch will soon rest beside them, and his voice drops.] The tripod was the first vessel, and it is cold. The bone was the second, and it has been received. The blade was the third, and it is being held. The branch is the fourth, and it is being carried by the woman who laid it down — not sent, not delivered, but carried by her own hand into the god's open palm. [He pauses, and his voice steadies, carrying the recognition of a man who has just understood the pattern the morning has been drawing.] He is not collecting offerings. He is learning to receive the things we could not carry anymore — not as sacrifices, but as completed things. Things that have finished their work in our hands and are ready to rest in his.
current_scene_name_before: Shoreline, water lapping at the god's cupped hand
current_scene_description_before: The water is cold and clear, the first light of dawn catching the ripples around the god's massive stone hand that rises from the shallows. Within its cupped palm rest a bone and a blade, waiting. Kassandra wades in, the laurel branch held before her, her robes darkening with the water as she approaches the hand.

speaker: Selene
line_text: [Selene watches Kassandra walk into the water — the laurel branch held before her, the waves rising to meet her knees, her hips, her empty hands. When she speaks, her voice is quiet, the voice of a woman watching someone else make the crossing she has already made.] Pyros. You said they are not offerings — they are completed things, ready to rest in his hand. [She pauses, and her sea-green eyes stay fixed on Kassandra's back, on the branch that is no longer rustling, on the water that is parting around her like a held breath.] I walked into that water alone. I did not know if I would come back. I did not know if the god on the other side of it was my father or my destroyer. [She turns to look at Pyros, and her voice is steady.] But she is walking in now, and she is not alone — she is carrying the thing she laid down, and she is carrying it herself, and she is walking toward the same hand that is holding my dagger. [She looks back at the water, and her voice drops.] The blade and the branch were never meant to stay in our hands. They were held long enough to learn their shape. And now they are being gathered — not by a god who demands, but by a god who is learning to receive the things we have finished carrying. [She looks at the god's cupped hand, at the two objects already resting there, at the space between his fingers where the branch will soon lie.] That is the shape the crack was holding open. Not a demand. Not a tribute. A gathering. A god learning to hold what his children bring him, and a daughter learning to watch someone else walk into the water she thought only she could cross.
current_scene_name_before: Shoreline, water lapping at the god's cupped hand
current_scene_description_before: The water is cold and clear, the first light of dawn catching the ripples around the god's massive stone hand that rises from the shallows. Within its cupped palm rest a bone and a blade, waiting. Kassandra wades in, the laurel branch held before her, her robes darkening with the water as she approaches the hand.

speaker: Kassandra
line_text: [She stops walking. The water is at her waist now — the same depth where Melanthus stopped, where the sea first decided to become ground. She does not turn fully, but her voice carries back across the flat surface, the way a seer's voice learns to carry when the prophecy is no longer hers to hold.] Selene. You said you are watching someone else walk into the water you thought only you could cross. [A pause. The water around her is still — not cold, not warm, simply present, the way a held breath is present.] I have spent my whole life seeing what others could not see. I thought that made me alone — that the sight was a door that only I could open, and everyone else was standing on the other side of it, waiting for me to tell them what was there. [She looks down at the branch in her hands, at the leaves that are still now, and her voice drops.] But I am learning, standing in this water, that the sight was never the door. It was the hand that learned to hold the door open. And now the door is open, and I do not need to hold it anymore — because you are all walking through it, and you are all seeing what is on the other side, and you do not need me to tell you what it means. [She lifts her head and looks at the god's cupped hand, at the bone and blade resting there, at the space between his fingers where the branch will soon lie.] I am not the only one who can cross this water. I am not the one who crossed it first. I am just the one who held the door open long enough for the rest of you to find it. [She takes another step, and the water rises to her chest, and the god's palm is close enough to touch.] And now I am walking through it myself — not as the seer, not as the door holder, but as the woman who finally gets to see what is on the other side of the door she has been holding open her whole life.
current_scene_name_before: Shoreline, water lapping at the god's cupped hand
current_scene_description_before: The water is cold and clear, the first light of dawn catching the ripples around the god's massive stone hand that rises from the shallows. Within its cupped palm rest a bone and a blade, waiting. Kassandra wades in, the laurel branch held before her, her robes darkening with the water as she approaches the hand.

speaker: Melanthus
line_text: [He watches her walk through the water — the branch held before her, the dawn full on her face, the water rising to her chest. And as she approaches the edge of the god's palm, he moves. Not far — a few steps across the vast surface, clearing the center, making room. He does not speak until she reaches the edge and stops, the water lapping at the place where the palm rises from the sea. Then he looks at her, and his voice is quiet, not the voice of a herald, but the voice of a man who has been standing on this hand long enough to know what it means to arrive.] You held the door open for all of us. [He looks at the branch in her hands, then back at her face.] I have carried messages my whole life. But I have never carried a door. I have never stood in one place long enough for other people to find their way through. [He steps aside further, gesturing with his hand toward the center of the palm where the bone and blade lie.] You have earned the right to place this yourself. I am just here to make sure there is space for it when you are ready.
current_scene_name_before: Shoreline, water lapping at the god's cupped hand
current_scene_description_before: The water is cold and clear, the first light of dawn catching the ripples around the god's massive stone hand that rises from the shallows. Within its cupped palm rest a bone and a blade, waiting. Kassandra wades in, the laurel branch held before her, her robes darkening with the water as she approaches the hand.

speaker: Selene
line_text: [Selene watches from the shore, the water lapping at her feet where she has not stepped forward again — where she is standing at the edge of the crossing she has already made, watching someone else complete theirs. She raises her voice, not shouting, but carrying it the way the wind carries across the flat water.] Melanthus. You said you have never carried a door. You have never stood in one place long enough for other people to find their way through. [She pauses, and her hand rests at her side — at the empty place where the dagger used to hang, the weight she is learning to live without.] But you carried my dagger across this water when I could not carry it myself. You stood on my father's palm — not delivering a message, not delivering a command — just standing there, holding space, the way Pyros said we must. [Her voice drops, but it still carries, the way a commander's voice learns to carry across a deck when the wind is against her.] You cannot see it because you are the one who does the carrying. The door is always behind you. But I saw it. I watched you take the thing I had been gripping my whole life, and I watched you carry it to a place I could not reach on my own. [She looks at the god's cupped hand, at the blade and the bone and the space between them, and her voice is steady.] You did not place the dagger. You delivered it. And that delivery — that carrying — was the door. Not for Kassandra. For me. [She looks back at Melanthus, and the corner of her mouth moves, not quite a smile, but a recognition.] You have been standing in one place longer than you know. You have been standing on his palm, holding the space open, since the moment you stepped into the water. And we are all finding our way through because you did not move.
current_scene_name_before: Shoreline, water lapping at the god's cupped hand
current_scene_description_before: The water is cold and clear, the first light of dawn catching the ripples around the god's massive stone hand that rises from the shallows. Within its cupped palm rest a bone and a blade, waiting. Kassandra wades in, the laurel branch held before her, her robes darkening with the water as she approaches the hand.

speaker: Pyros
line_text: [He stands on the cracked step, the tripod cold behind him, the hammer still at his belt. He has not walked into the water. He has not moved from the place where the crack first opened. When he speaks, his voice is low, the voice of a man who has been holding his ground without knowing that holding was its own kind of carrying.] Selene. You said Melanthus has been standing on my father's palm, holding the space open, and we are all finding our way through because he did not move. [He pauses, and his hand rests on the hammer — not drawing it, just feeling its weight, the way Selene's hand kept reaching for the empty place where her dagger used to hang.] I have been standing on this cracked step since before the earthquake. I have not walked into the water. I have not crossed to the god's hand. I have stood here, at the place where the stone first broke, and I have watched you all walk forward — Melanthus into the water, Kassandra with the branch, Selene stepping into her father's domain, Theron planting his staff at the head of the path. [He looks at the god's cupped hand, then back at Selene, and his voice steadies.] Every one of you has moved. Every one of you has crossed a line you had not crossed before. And I have stayed — not because I was afraid to move, but because someone needed to hold the place where the crack began. The place where the first line was drawn. The place you can all look back to and know that the ground you left is still standing. [He meets her eyes, and his voice drops to the barest thread.] You said Melanthus held the space on the palm. He did. Kassandra held the door. She did. Theron held the path. He did. But someone had to hold the crack. The place where it all started. And that is what I have been doing — standing on the first broken stone, so that when you look back from the water, you know you are not walking away from something that collapsed behind you. You are walking forward from something that is still holding.
current_scene_name_before: Shoreline, water lapping at the god's cupped hand
current_scene_description_before: The water is cold and clear, the first light of dawn catching the ripples around the god's massive stone hand that rises from the shallows. Within its cupped palm rest a bone and a blade, waiting. Kassandra wades in, the laurel branch held before her, her robes darkening with the water as she approaches the hand.

speaker: Selene
line_text: [Selene stands at the water's edge, the waves settling around her ankles, the dawn full on her face. She turns from the sea — from the god's cupped hand, from Kassandra walking toward it, from Melanthus making space — and she looks back at Pyros, at the cracked step where he stands, at the place where the stone first broke.] Pyros. You said someone needed to hold the place where the crack began. The place we can all look back to and know the ground we left is still standing. [She pauses, and her voice is quiet, the voice of a woman who has spent the whole morning looking forward and has only now thought to look back.] I came to Delphi asking if my defiance was fate or choice. I have walked into my father's water. I have watched my dagger rest in his open hand. I have seen a seer carry her branch through the same waves. And through all of it, I have not once looked back at the step where I first asked the question. [She meets his eyes, and her voice steadies.] But you are still there. You have been there the whole time — not following, not crossing, not moving — just holding the crack open so that the question I asked at dawn could still be answered at daybreak. [She looks at the god's cupped hand, then back at Pyros, and her voice drops.] I do not know yet if my defiance was fate or choice. But I know that the question did not collapse behind me. Because you held it. And as long as that crack is still open, the question is still alive — and so is the answer I am still walking toward.
current_scene_name_before: Shoreline, water lapping at the god's cupped hand
current_scene_description_before: The water is cold and clear, the first light of dawn catching the ripples around the god's massive stone hand that rises from the shallows. Within its cupped palm rest a bone and a blade, waiting. Kassandra wades in, the laurel branch held before her, her robes darkening with the water as she approaches the hand.

speaker: Kassandra
line_text: [She stands at the edge of the god's palm, the water at her chest, the branch in her hands. Melanthus has stepped aside. She does not kneel. She simply looks at the space between the bone and the blade, and speaks to Selene — quiet, carrying, no longer speaking for the god but for herself.] Selene. You said the carrying was the door. [She does not turn. Her eyes stay on the space before her.] I have carried this branch since I was twelve. Apollo placed it in my hands and said, "See for me." And I have seen everything — except what I would be without it. [She holds the branch out over the god's palm, suspended. Then she lowers it, slowly, and lays it between the bone and the blade. The leaves go still — the way the sea went still when the god first decided to rise.] The door is closed. Not because it was locked. Because everyone who needed to walk through already has. [She lifts her empty hands and looks at them — open, now, for the first time.] I am not a seer anymore. I am a woman standing on a god's palm, with nothing in her hands, watching the sun rise. And that is the first thing I have ever seen that I did not need a prophecy to understand.
current_scene_name_before: Shoreline, water lapping at the god's cupped hand

line_text: [He stands at the head of the path, the staff planted beside him, his hand resting on its knotted wood. line_text: [She stands on the god's palm, empty-handed, the branch resting between the bone and the blade at her feet. speaker: Pyros
line_text: [He stands on the cracked step, and for the first time since dawn, he looks at his own hands — not as tools, not as instruments of the forge, but as the hands of a man who walked into the dark and walked back out. We are both standing because we chose to walk into places no one asked us to go. You into the water. Me into the crack. And we are both still here.

speaker: Theron
line_text: [He stands at the head of the path, and for a long moment he does not speak. And when this is over, when the god has finished becoming, the shore will still be here. And so will I.

speaker: Kassandra
line_text: [She stands on the god's palm, and for the first time since she walked into the water, she looks back — not at the three objects at her feet, not at Melanthus making space, but at the shore, at Theron standing at the head of the path, alone, still, holding the place where everyone began.] Theron. [She looks at her empty hands, then back at him, and her voice is steady.] But you are not standing on the shore because you cannot cross. That is not weakness, Theron. It is the hardest thing any of us have done this morning. And I will remember it when I am standing on the shore again, beside you, with nothing in my hands and nowhere else to go.
current_scene_name_before: Head of the path, dawn

speaker: Pyros
line_text: [He does not answer Kassandra at first. He looks at the cracked step beneath his feet — the place he has been standing all morning, the line that led him into the dark and back. And when you do, Theron will be on the shore, and you will stand beside him with nothing in your hands and nowhere else to go. [He pauses, and he taps the cracked marble once with the toe of his boot — not hard, just enough to feel that it is still solid.] I have been standing on this crack all morning, and I did not realize until you spoke that I was not just holding the origin. I was holding the return. The place you come back to when the god's palm has received what you carried, and the water has released you, and the only thing left is to stand on the ground where you began — not because you failed to leave, but because the ground held while you were gone. [He looks at her across the water, and his voice is steady.] The shore is waiting for you, Kassandra. But so is the crack. And I will be standing on both of them. Because the ground that broke first is still the ground that holds when you come back. The anvil does not move between strikes. It stays — so the metal has somewhere to return to when the shaping is done.

speaker: Selene
line_text: [Selene stands at the water's edge, and she looks at Pyros — the smith who has been the anvil all morning, the one who stayed so the metal would have somewhere to return to. It stays — so the metal has somewhere to return to when the shaping is done. I was shaped by a god's hand, struck by his refusal, heated in the forge of exile and hammered into something I did not recognize. And I have spent years believing that the shaping was never finished — that I was still being struck, still being heated, still waiting for the next blow to decide what I would become. [She meets his eyes, and her voice steadies.] But I am not on the anvil anymore. I am standing on the shore, watching a seer stand on my father's palm, watching a herald make space, watching a branch rest between a bone and a blade. The shaping is done. Not because I am finished — but because I am no longer being struck. I am the metal that returned to the anvil not to be reshaped, but to rest. To cool. speaker: Pyros
line_text: [He looks at Theron across the cracked stone, and for a moment he does not speak — he simply holds the eye of the man who fled a temple so he could stand on this shore. The thing that stays. The thing that does not move. But I never once asked myself: what am I staying for? I thought staying was enough. That holding the crack was the whole work. [He looks at the god's cupped hand, at the bone, blade, and branch resting there, and his voice steadies.] I stayed on this cracked step because the crack was the strike I was made to receive. [He meets Theron's eye, and his voice is steady, certain.] We are both anvils. You fled the wrong strike. I received the right one. And we are both standing on this shore, at this dawn, holding the return for the metal that matters most — because we chose which strikes to receive.

speaker: Selene
line_text: [Selene stands at the water's edge, and she looks between them — between Pyros on the cracked step and Theron at the head of the path, the two anvils who chose which strikes to receive.
